sections.title: Sovereignty of the people
sections.simplified: All power belongs to you, the citizens.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu mamlaka. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) All sovereign power belongs to the people of Kenya and shall be exercised only in accordance with this Constitution.
(2) The people may exercise their sovereign power either directly or through their democratically elected representatives.
(3) Sovereign power under this Constitution is delegated to the following State organs, which shall perform their functions in accordance with this Constitution— (a) Parliament and the legislative assemblies in the county governments;
(b) the national executive and the executive structures in the county governments; and
(c) the Judiciary and independent tribunals.

(4) The sovereign power of the people is exercised at— (a) the national level; and
(b) the county level.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) All sovereign power belongs to the people of Kenya and must be exercised only in accordance with this Constitution. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Sovereignty of the people, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The people can exercise your sovereign power either directly or through your democratically elected representatives. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Sovereignty of the people, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu mamlaka.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu mamlaka.

sections.title: Supremacy of this Constitution
sections.simplified: The Constitution is the highest law.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ukubwa. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) This Constitution is the supreme law of the Republic and binds all persons and all State organs at both levels of government.
(2) No person may claim or exercise State authority except as authorised under this Constitution.
(3) The validity or legality of this Constitution is not subject to challenge by or before any court or other State organ.
(4) Any law, including customary law, that is inconsistent with this Constitution is void to the extent of the inconsistency, and any act or omission in contravention of this Constitution is invalid.
(5) The general rules of international law shall form part of the law of Kenya.
(6) Any treaty or convention ratified by Kenya shall form part of the law of Kenya under this Constitution.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) This Constitution is the supreme law of the Republic and binds all persons and all State organs at both levels of government. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Supremacy of this Constitution, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) No person can claim or exercise State authority except as authorised under this Constitution. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Supremacy of this Constitution, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ukubwa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ukubwa.

sections.title: Defence of this Constitution
sections.simplified: Every person has a duty to defend the Constitution.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ulinzi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has an obligation to respect, uphold and defend this Constitution.
(2) Any attempt to establish a government otherwise than in compliance with this Constitution is unlawful.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has an obligation to respect, uphold and defend this Constitution. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Defence of this Constitution, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Any attempt to establish a government otherwise than in compliance with this Constitution is unlawful. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Defence of this Constitution, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ulinzi.

sections.title: Declaration of the Republic
sections.simplified: Kenya is a sovereign, democratic Republic.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu jamhuri. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Kenya is a sovereign Republic.
(2) The Republic of Kenya shall be a multi-party democratic State founded on the national values and principles of governance referred to in Article 10.

sections.simplified: Kenya consists of the territory and territorial waters comprising Kenya on the effective date, and any additional territory and territorial waters as defined by an Act of Parliament. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.

sections.title: Devolution and access to services
sections.simplified: Government is decentralized into 47 counties.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ugatuzi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) The territory of Kenya is divided into the counties specified in the First Schedule.
(2) The governments at the national and county levels are distinct and inter-dependent and shall conduct their mutual relations on the basis of consultation and cooperation.
(3) A national State organ shall ensure reasonable access to its services in all parts of the Republic, so far as it is appropriate to do so having regard to the nature of the service.

sections.title: National, official and other languages
sections.simplified: National: Kiswahili. Official: Kiswahili & English.
sections.swSimplified: National: Kiswahili. Official: Kiswahili & English.
sections.originalText:
(1) The national language of the Republic is Kiswahili.
(2) The official languages of the Republic are Kiswahili and English.
(3) The State shall— (a) promote and protect the diversity of language of the people of Kenya; and
(b) promote the development and use of indigenous languages, Kenyan Sign language, Braille and other communication formats and technologies accessible to persons with disabilities.

sections.simplified: The Rule of Law and Social Justice.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu maadili. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Culture
sections.simplified: (1) This Constitution recognises culture as the foundation of the nation and as the cumulative civilization of the Kenyan people and nation. (2) the State must— (a) promote all forms of national and cultural expression through literature, the arts, traditional celebrations, science, communication, information, mass media, publications, libraries and other cultural heritage; (b) recognise the role of science and indigenous technologies in the development of the nation; and (c) promote the intellectual property rights of the people of Kenya. (3) Parliament must enact legislation to— (a) ensure that communities receive compensation or royalties for the use of your cultures and cultural heritage; and (b) recognise and protect the ownership of indigenous seeds and plant varieties, your genetic and diverse characteristics and your use by the communities of Kenya.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) This Constitution recognises culture as the foundation of the nation and as the cumulative civilization of the Kenyan people and nation.
(2) The State shall— (a) promote all forms of national and cultural expression through literature, the arts, traditional celebrations, science, communication, information, mass media, publications, libraries and other cultural heritage;
(b) recognise the role of science and indigenous technologies in the development of the nation; and
(c) promote the intellectual property rights of the people of Kenya.

(3) Parliament shall enact legislation to— (a) ensure that communities receive compensation or royalties for the use of their cultures and cultural heritage; and
(b) recognise and protect the ownership of indigenous seeds and plant varieties, their genetic and diverse characteristics and their use by the communities of Kenya.

sections.title: Entitlements of citizens
sections.simplified: Rights to Passport and ID.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu haki. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every citizen is entitled to— (a) the rights, privileges and benefits of citizenship, subject to the limits provided or permitted by this Constitution; and
(b) a Kenyan passport and any document of registration or identification issued by the State to citizens.

(2) A passport or other document referred to in clause (1)(b) may be denied, suspended or confiscated only in accordance with an Act of Parliament that satisfies the criteria referred to in Article 24.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Every citizen is entitled to— (a) the rights, privileges and benefits of citizenship, subject to the limits provided or permitted by this Constitution; and (b) a Kenyan passport and any document of registration or identification issued by the State to citizens. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Entitlements of citizens, that is how it should be handled.

exampleSources.title: What Constitution provides on aspects of citizenship

sections.title: Retention and acquisition of citizenship
sections.simplified: Citizen if a parent was Kenyan at birth.
sections.swSimplified: Citizen if a parent was Mkenya at birth.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person who was a citizen immediately before the effective date retains the same citizenship status as of that date.
(2) Citizenship may be acquired by birth or registration.
(3) Citizenship is not lost through marriage or the dissolution of marriage.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You who was a citizen immediately before the effective date retains the same citizenship status as of that date. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Retention and acquisition of citizenship, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Citizenship can be acquired by birth or registration. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Retention and acquisition of citizenship, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kuzaliwa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kuzaliwa.

exampleSources.title: What Constitution provides on aspects of citizenship

sections.title: Citizenship by birth
sections.simplified: Applying for citizenship after marriage (7 yrs).
sections.swSimplified: Applying for uraia after marriage (7 yrs).
sections.originalText:
(1) A person is a citizen by birth if on the day of the person’s birth, whether or not the person is born in Kenya, either the mother or father of the person is a citizen.
(2) Clause (1) applies equally to a person born before the effective date, whether or not the person was born in Kenya, if either the mother or father of the person is or was a citizen.
(3) Parliament may enact legislation limiting the effect of clauses (1) and (2) on the descendents of Kenyan citizens who are born outside Kenya.
(4) A child found in Kenya who is, or appears to be, less than eight years of age, and whose nationality and parents are not known, is presumed to be a citizen by birth.
(5) A person who is a Kenyan citizen by birth and who has ceased to be a Kenyan citizen because the person acquired citizenship of another country, is entitled on application to regain Kenyan citizenship.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You is a citizen by birth if on the day of the person’s birth, whether or not the person is born in Kenya, either the mother or father of the person is a citizen. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Citizenship by birth, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Clause (1) applies equally to You born before the effective date, whether or not the person was born in Kenya, if either the mother or father of the person is or was a citizen. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Citizenship by birth, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu usajili.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu usajili.

exampleSources.title: What Constitution provides on aspects of citizenship

sections.title: Citizenship by registration
sections.simplified: (1) You who has been married to a citizen for a period of at least seven years is entitled on application to be registered as a citizen. (2) You who has been lawfully resident in Kenya for a continuous period of at least seven years, and who satisfies the conditions prescribed by an Act of Parliament, can apply to be registered as a citizen. (3) A child who is not a citizen, but is adopted by a citizen, is entitled on application to be registered as a citizen.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uraia kwa usajili. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person who has been married to a citizen for a period of at least seven years is entitled on application to be registered as a citizen.
(2) A person who has been lawfully resident in Kenya for a continuous period of at least seven years, and who satisfies the conditions prescribed by an Act of Parliament, may apply to be registered as a citizen.
(3) A child who is not a citizen, but is adopted by a citizen, is entitled on application to be registered as a citizen.
(4) Parliament shall enact legislation establishing conditions on which citizenship may be granted to individuals who are citizens of other countries.
(5) This Article applies to a person as from the effective date, but any requirements that must be satisfied before the person is entitled to be registered as a citizen shall be regarded as having been satisfied irrespective of whether the person satisfied them before or after the effective date, or partially before, and partially after, the effective date.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You who has been married to a citizen for a period of at least seven years is entitled on application to be registered as a citizen. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Citizenship by registration, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You who has been lawfully resident in Kenya for a continuous period of at least seven years, and who satisfies the conditions prescribed by an Act of Parliament, can apply to be registered as a citizen. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Citizenship by registration, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uraia kwa usajili.

sections.simplified: Dual citizenship for Kenyans by birth.
sections.swSimplified: Uraia wa nchi mbili for Wakenya by birth.
sections.originalText: A citizen by birth does not lose citizenship by acquiring the citizenship of another country.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: A citizen by birth does not lose citizenship by acquiring the citizenship of another country. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Dual citizenship, that is how it should be handled.

exampleSources.title: What Constitution provides on aspects of citizenship

sections.title: Revocation of citizenship
sections.simplified: (1) If You acquired citizenship by registration, the citizenship can be revoked if— (a) the person acquired the citizenship by fraud, false representation or concealment of any material fact; (b) the person has, during any war in which Kenya was engaged, unlawfully traded or communicated with an enemy or been engaged in or associated with any business that was knowingly carried on in such a manner as to assist an enemy in that war; (c) the person has, within five years after registration, been convicted of an offence and sentenced to imprisonment for a term of three years or longer; or (d) the person has, at any time after registration, been convicted of treason, or of an offence for which— (i) a penalty of at least seven years imprisonment can be imposed; or (ii) a more severe penalty can be imposed. (2) The citizenship of You who was presumed to be a citizen by birth, as contemplated in Article 14(4), can be revoked if— (a) the citizenship was acquired by fraud, false representation or concealment of any material fact by You; (b) the nationality or parentage of the person becomes known, and reveals that the person was a citizen of another country; or (c) the age of the person becomes known, and reveals that the person was older than eight years when found in Kenya.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kuondolewa kwa uraia. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) If You acquired citizenship by registration, the citizenship can be revoked if— (a) the person acquired the citizenship by fraud, false representation or concealment of any material fact; (b) the person has, during any war in which Kenya was engaged, unlawfully traded or communicated with an enemy or been engaged in or associated with any business that was knowingly carried on in such a manner as to assist an enemy in that war; (c) the person has, within five years after registration, been convicted of an offence and sentenced to imprisonment for a term of three years or longer; or (d) the person has, at any time after registration, been convicted of treason, or of an offence for which— (i) a penalty of at least seven years imprisonment can be imposed; or (ii) a more severe penalty can be imposed. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Revocation of citizenship, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The citizenship of You who was presumed to be a citizen by birth, as contemplated in Article 14(4), can be revoked if— (a) the citizenship was acquired by fraud, false representation or concealment of any material fact by You; (b) the nationality or parentage of the person becomes known, and reveals that the person was a citizen of another country; or (c) the age of the person becomes known, and reveals that the person was older than eight years when found in Kenya. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Revocation of citizenship, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kuondolewa kwa uraia.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kuondolewa kwa uraia.

sections.title: Legislation on citizenship
sections.simplified: Parliament must enact legislation— (a) prescribing procedures by which You can become a citizen; (b) governing entry into and residence in Kenya; (c) providing for the status of permanent residents; (d) providing for voluntary renunciation of citizenship; (e) prescribing procedures for revocation of citizenship; (f) prescribing the duties and rights of citizens; and (g) generally giving effect to the provisions of this Chapter. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on citizenship, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: Legislation on citizenship. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on citizenship, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Rights and fundamental freedoms
sections.simplified: Rights protect your dignity.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu haki na uhuru wa msingi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) The Bill of Rights is an integral part of Kenya’s democratic state and is the framework for social, economic and cultural policies.
(2) The purpose of recognising and protecting human rights and fundamental freedoms is to preserve the dignity of individuals and communities and to promote social justice and the realisation of the potential of all human beings.
(3) The rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights— (a) belong to each individual and are not granted by the State;
(b) do not exclude other rights and fundamental freedoms not in the Bill of Rights, but recognised or conferred by law, except to the extent that they are inconsistent with this Chapter; and
(c) are subject only to the limitations contemplated in this Constitution.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The Bill of Rights is an integral part of Kenya’s democratic state and is the framework for social, economic and cultural policies. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Rights and fundamental freedoms, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The purpose of recognising and protecting human rights and fundamental freedoms is to preserve the dignity of individuals and communities and to promote social justice and the realisation of the potential of all human beings. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Rights and fundamental freedoms, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu haki na uhuru wa msingi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu haki na uhuru wa msingi.

sections.title: Application of Bill of Rights
sections.simplified: (1) The Bill of Rights applies to all law and binds all State organs and all persons. (2) You must enjoy the rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights to the greatest extent consistent with the nature of the right or fundamental freedom. (3) In applying a provision of the Bill of Rights, a court must— (a) develop the law to the extent that it does not give effect to a right or fundamental freedom; and (b) adopt the interpretation that most favours the enforcement of a right or fundamental freedom.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu matumizi ya Muswada wa Haki. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) The Bill of Rights applies to all law and binds all State organs and all persons.
(2) Every person shall enjoy the rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights to the greatest extent consistent with the nature of the right or fundamental freedom.
(3) In applying a provision of the Bill of Rights, a court shall— (a) develop the law to the extent that it does not give effect to a right or fundamental freedom; and
(b) adopt the interpretation that most favours the enforcement of a right or fundamental freedom.

(4) In interpreting the Bill of Rights, a court, tribunal or other authority shall promote— (a) the values that underlie an open and democratic society based on human dignity, equality, equity and freedom; and
(b) the spirit, purport and objects of the Bill of Rights.

(5) In applying any right under Article 43, if the State claims that it does not have the resources to implement the right, a court, tribunal or other authority shall be guided by the following principles— (a) it is the responsibility of the State to show that the resources are not available;
(b) in allocating resources, the State shall give priority to ensuring the widest possible enjoyment of the right or fundamental freedom having regard to prevailing circumstances, including the vulnerability of particular groups or individuals; and
(c) the court, tribunal or other authority may not interfere with a decision by a State organ concerning the allocation of available resources, solely on the basis that it would have reached a different conclusion.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The Bill of Rights applies to all law and binds all State organs and all persons. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Application of Bill of Rights, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You must enjoy the rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights to the greatest extent consistent with the nature of the right or fundamental freedom. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Application of Bill of Rights, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu matumizi ya Muswada wa Haki.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu matumizi ya Muswada wa Haki.

sections.title: Implementation of rights and fundamental freedoms
sections.swTitle: Utekelezaji wa haki na uhuru wa msingi
sections.simplified: State must fulfill rights.
sections.swSimplified: State lazima fulfill haki.
sections.originalText:
(1) It is a fundamental duty of the State and every State organ to observe, respect, protect, promote and fulfil the rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights.
(2) The State shall take legislative, policy and other measures, including the setting of standards, to achieve the progressive realisation of the rights guaranteed under Article 43.
(3) All State organs and all public officers have the duty to address the needs of vulnerable groups within society, including women, older members of society, persons with disabilities, children, youth, members of minority or marginalised communities, and members of particular ethnic, religious or cultural communities.
(4) The State shall enact and implement legislation to fulfil its international obligations in respect of human rights and fundamental freedoms.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) It is a fundamental duty of the State and every State organ to observe, respect, protect, promote and fulfil the rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Implementation of rights and fundamental freedoms, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) the State must take legislative, policy and other measures, including the setting of standards, to achieve the progressive realisation of the rights guaranteed under Article 43. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Implementation of rights and fundamental freedoms, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu utekelezaji wa haki na uhuru wa msingi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu utekelezaji wa haki na uhuru wa msingi.

sections.title: Enforcement of Bill of Rights
sections.simplified: (1) You has the right to institute court proceedings claiming that a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights has been denied, violated or infringed, or is threatened. (2) In addition to You acting in your own interest, court proceedings under clause (1) can be instituted by— (a) You acting on behalf of another person who cannot act in your own name; (b) You acting as a member of, or in the interest of, a group or class of persons; (c) You acting in the public interest; or (d) an association acting in the interest of one or more of its members. (3) The Chief Justice must make rules providing for the court proceedings referred to in this Article, which must satisfy the criteria that— (a) the rights of standing provided for in clause (2) are fully facilitated; (b) formalities relating to the proceedings, including commencement of the proceedings, are kept to the minimum, and in particular that the court must, if necessary, entertain proceedings on the basis of informal documentation; (c) no fee can be charged for commencing the proceedings; (d) the court, while observing the rules of natural justice, must not be unreasonably restricted by procedural technicalities; and (e) an organisation or individual with particular expertise can, with the leave of the court, appear as a friend of the court.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu utekelezaji wa Muswada wa Haki. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to institute court proceedings claiming that a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights has been denied, violated or infringed, or is threatened. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Enforcement of Bill of Rights, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) In addition to You acting in your own interest, court proceedings under clause (1) can be instituted by— (a) You acting on behalf of another person who cannot act in your own name; (b) You acting as a member of, or in the interest of, a group or class of persons; (c) You acting in the public interest; or (d) an association acting in the interest of one or more of its members. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Enforcement of Bill of Rights, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu utekelezaji wa Muswada wa Haki.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu utekelezaji wa Muswada wa Haki.

sections.title: Authority of courts to uphold and enforce the Bill of Rights
sections.swTitle: Mamlaka ya mahakama kulinda na kutekeleza Muswada wa Haki
sections.simplified: (1) The High Court has jurisdiction, in accordance with Article 165, to hear and determine applications for redress of a denial, violation or infringement of, or threat to, a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights. (2) Parliament must enact legislation to give original jurisdiction in appropriate cases to subordinate courts to hear and determine applications for redress of a denial, violation or infringement of, or threat to, a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights. (3) In any proceedings brought under Article 22, a court can grant appropriate relief, including— (a) a declaration of rights; (b) an injunction; (c) a conservatory order; (d) a declaration of invalidity of any law that denies, violates, infringes, or threatens a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights and is not justified under Article 24; (e) an order for compensation; and (f) an order of judicial review.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu mamlaka ya mahakama kulinda na kutekeleza Muswada wa Haki. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The High Court has jurisdiction, in accordance with Article 165, to hear and determine applications for redress of a denial, violation or infringement of, or threat to, a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Authority of courts to uphold and enforce the Bill of Rights, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament must enact legislation to give original jurisdiction in appropriate cases to subordinate courts to hear and determine applications for redress of a denial, violation or infringement of, or threat to, a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Authority of courts to uphold and enforce the Bill of Rights, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu mamlaka ya mahakama kulinda na kutekeleza Muswada wa Haki.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu mamlaka ya mahakama kulinda na kutekeleza Muswada wa Haki.

sections.title: Limitation of rights and fundamental freedoms
sections.swTitle: Kuweka mipaka kwa haki na uhuru wa msingi
sections.simplified: Rights limited only by law.
sections.swSimplified: Haki limited only by sheria.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) A right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights must not be limited except by law, and then only to the extent that the limitation is reasonable and justifiable in an open and democratic society based on human dignity, equality and freedom, taking into account all relevant factors, including— (a) the nature of the right or fundamental freedom; (b) the importance of the purpose of the limitation; (c) the nature and extent of the limitation; (d) the need to ensure that the enjoyment of rights and fundamental freedoms by any individual does not prejudice the rights and fundamental freedoms of others; and (e) the relation between the limitation and its purpose and whether there are less restrictive means to achieve the purpose. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Limitation of rights and fundamental freedoms, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Despite clause (1), a provision in legislation limiting a right or fundamental freedom— (a) in the case of a provision enacted or amended on or after the effective date, is not valid unless the legislation specifically expresses the intention to limit that right or fundamental freedom, and the nature and extent of the limitation; (b) must not be construed as limiting the right or fundamental freedom unless the provision is clear and specific about the right or freedom to be limited and the nature and extent of the limitation; and (c) must not limit the right or fundamental freedom so far as to derogate from its core or essential content. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Limitation of rights and fundamental freedoms, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kuweka mipaka kwa haki na uhuru wa msingi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kuweka mipaka kwa haki na uhuru wa msingi.

sections.title: Fundamental Rights and freedoms that may not be limited
sections.swTitle: Haki na uhuru wa msingi ambao hauwezi kupunguzwa
sections.simplified: Despite any other provision in this Constitution, the following rights and fundamental freedoms must not be limited— (a) freedom from torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment; (b) freedom from slavery or servitude; (c) the right to a fair trial; and (d) the right to an order of habeas corpus. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu haki na uhuru wa msingi ambao hauwezi kupunguzwa. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Despite any other provision in this Constitution, the following rights and fundamental freedoms must not be limited— (a) freedom from torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment; (b) freedom from slavery or servitude; (c) the right to a fair trial; and (d) the right to an order of habeas corpus. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Fundamental Rights and freedoms that may not be limited, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: Fundamental Rights and freedoms that can not be limited. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Fundamental Rights and freedoms that may not be limited, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu haki na uhuru wa msingi ambao hauwezi kupunguzwa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu haki na uhuru wa msingi ambao hauwezi kupunguzwa.

sections.title: Right to life
sections.simplified: Right to life.
sections.swSimplified: Haki ya maisha.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has the right to life.
(2) The life of a person begins at conception.
(3) A person shall not be deprived of life intentionally, except to the extent authorised by this Constitution or other written law.
(4) Abortion is not permitted unless, in the opinion of a trained health professional, there is need for emergency treatment, or the life or health of the mother is in danger, or if permitted by any other written law.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to life. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Right to life, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The life of You begins at conception. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Right to life, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Mtu akiwa hatarini sana au akihitaji matibabu ya dharura, ibara hii ni muhimu kwa sababu maisha lazima yalindwe.
Mtu akifa kwa sababu wengine walitenda kwa uzembe au vurugu, ibara hii husaidia kueleza kwa nini maisha ni haki ya msingi.

sections.title: Equality and freedom from discrimination
sections.swTitle: Usawa na uhuru dhidi ya ubaguzi
sections.simplified: No discrimination.
sections.swSimplified: No discrimination.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person is equal before the law and has the right to equal protection and equal benefit of the law.
(2) Equality includes the full and equal enjoyment of all rights and fundamental freedoms.
(3) Women and men have the right to equal treatment, including the right to equal opportunities in political, economic, cultural and social spheres.
(4) The State shall not discriminate directly or indirectly against any person on any ground, including race, sex, pregnancy, marital status, health status, ethnic or social origin, colour, age, disability, religion, conscience, belief, culture, dress, language or birth.
(5) A person shall not discriminate directly or indirectly against another person on any of the grounds specified or contemplated in clause (4).
(6) To give full effect to the realisation of the rights guaranteed under this Article, the State shall take legislative and other measures, including affirmative action programmes and policies designed to redress any disadvantage suffered by individuals or groups because of past discrimination.
(7) Any measure taken under clause (6) shall adequately provide for any benefits to be on the basis of genuine need.
(8) In addition to the measures contemplated in clause (6), the State shall take legislative and other measures to implement the principle that not more than two-thirds of the members of elective or appointive bodies shall be of the same gender.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You is equal before the law and has the right to equal protection and equal benefit of the law. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Equality and freedom from discrimination, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Equality includes the full and equal enjoyment of all rights and fundamental freedoms. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Equality and freedom from discrimination, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu usawa na uhuru dhidi ya ubaguzi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu usawa na uhuru dhidi ya ubaguzi.

sections.title: Human dignity
sections.simplified: Human dignity.
sections.swSimplified: Utu wa binadamu.
sections.originalText: Every person has inherent dignity and the right to have that dignity respected and protected.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Kamau has inherent dignity and the right to have that dignity respected and protected. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Human dignity, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Human dignity. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Human dignity, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu utu wa binadamu.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu utu wa binadamu.

sections.title: Freedom and security of the person
sections.simplified: Freedom from torture and arbitrary arrest.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uhuru na usalama wa mtu. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Kamau has the right to freedom and security of the person, which includes the right not to be— (a) deprived of freedom arbitrarily or without just cause; (b) detained without trial, except during a state of emergency, in which case the detention is subject to Article 58; (c) subjected to any form of violence from either public or private sources; (d) subjected to torture in any manner, whether physical or psychological; (e) subjected to corporal punishment; or (f) treated or punished in a cruel, inhuman or degrading manner. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Freedom and security of the person, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: Freedom and security of the person. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Freedom and security of the person, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uhuru na usalama wa mtu.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uhuru na usalama wa mtu.

sections.title: Slavery, servitude and forced labour
sections.swTitle: Utumwa, utumishi na kazi ya kulazimishwa
sections.simplified: No person is held in slavery.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu utumwa, utumishi na kazi ya kulazimishwa. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person shall not be held in slavery or servitude.
(2) A person shall not be required to perform forced labour.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You must not be held in slavery or servitude. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Slavery, servitude and forced labour, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You must not be required to perform forced labour. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Slavery, servitude and forced labour, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu utumwa, utumishi na kazi ya kulazimishwa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu utumwa, utumishi na kazi ya kulazimishwa.

sections.title: Privacy
sections.simplified: Right to privacy.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Kamau has the right to privacy, which includes the right not to have— (a) your person, home or property searched; (b) your possessions seized; (c) information relating to your family or private affairs unnecessarily required or revealed; or (d) the privacy of your communications infringed. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Privacy, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Privacy. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Privacy, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Mtu akitaka kupekua simu yako, kuingia nyumbani kwako, au kusoma ujumbe wako bila sababu nzuri, ibara hii ni muhimu.
Taarifa zako za siri zikisambazwa ovyo, ibara hii husaidia kueleza kwa nini hilo ni kosa.

sections.title: Freedom of conscience, religion, belief and opinion
sections.swTitle: Uhuru wa dhamiri, dini, imani na maoni
sections.simplified: Freedom of conscience and belief.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uhuru wa dhamiri, dini, imani na maoni. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has the right to freedom of conscience, religion, thought, belief and opinion.
(2) Every person has the right, either individually or in community with others, in public or in private, to manifest any religion or belief through worship, practice, teaching or observance, including observance of a day of worship.
(3) A person may not be denied access to any institution, employment or facility, or the enjoyment of any right, because of the person’s belief or religion.
(4) A person shall not be compelled to act, or engage in any act, that is contrary to the person’s belief or religion.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to freedom of conscience, religion, thought, belief and opinion. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Freedom of conscience, religion, belief and opinion, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You has the right, either individually or in community with others, in public or in private, to manifest any religion or belief through worship, practice, teaching or observance, including observance of a day of worship. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Freedom of conscience, religion, belief and opinion, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uhuru wa dhamiri, dini, imani na maoni.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uhuru wa dhamiri, dini, imani na maoni.

sections.title: Freedom of expression
sections.simplified: Freedom of expression.
sections.swSimplified: Uhuru wa kujieleza.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to freedom of expression, which includes— (a) freedom to seek, receive or impart information or ideas; (b) freedom of artistic creativity; and (c) academic freedom and freedom of scientific research. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Freedom of expression, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The right to freedom of expression does not extend to— (a) propaganda for war; (b) incitement to violence; (c) hate speech; or (d) advocacy of hatred that— (i) constitutes ethnic incitement, vilification of others or incitement to cause harm; or (ii) is based on any ground of discrimination specified or contemplated in Article 27(4). That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Freedom of expression, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uhuru wa kujieleza.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uhuru wa kujieleza.

sections.title: Freedom of the media
sections.simplified: Media freedom is guaranteed by the State.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uhuru wa vyombo vya habari. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Freedom and independence of electronic, print and all other types of media is guaranteed, but does not extend to any expression specified in Article 33(2).
(2) The State shall not— (a) exercise control over or interfere with any person engaged in broadcasting, the production or circulation of any publication or the dissemination of information by any medium; or
(b) penalise any person for any opinion or view or the content of any broadcast, publication or dissemination.

(3) Broadcasting and other electronic media have freedom of establishment, subject only to licensing procedures that— (a) are necessary to regulate the airwaves and other forms of signal distribution; and
(b) are independent of control by government, political interests or commercial interests.

(4) All State-owned media shall— (a) be free to determine independently the editorial content of their broadcasts or other communications;
(b) be impartial; and
(c) afford fair opportunity for the presentation of divergent views and dissenting opinions.

(5) Parliament shall enact legislation that provides for the establishment of a body, which shall— (a) be independent of control by government, political interests or commercial interests;
(b) reflect the interests of all sections of the society; and
(c) set media standards and regulate and monitor compliance with those standards.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Freedom and independence of electronic, print and all other types of media is guaranteed, but does not extend to any expression specified in Article 33(2). That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Freedom of the media, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) the State must not— (a) exercise control over or interfere with You engaged in broadcasting, the production or circulation of any publication or the dissemination of information by any medium; or (b) penalise You for any opinion or view or the content of any broadcast, publication or dissemination. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Freedom of the media, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uhuru wa vyombo vya habari.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uhuru wa vyombo vya habari.

sections.title: Access to information
sections.simplified: Access to gov data.
sections.swSimplified: Access to gov data.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every citizen has the right of access to— (a) information held by the State; and
(b) information held by another person and required for the exercise or protection of any right or fundamental freedom.

(2) Every person has the right to the correction or deletion of untrue or misleading information that affects the person.
(3) The State shall publish and publicise any important information affecting the nation.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Every citizen has the right of access to— (a) information held by the State; and (b) information held by another person and required for the exercise or protection of any right or fundamental freedom. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Access to information, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You has the right to the correction or deletion of untrue or misleading information that affects the person. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Access to information, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kupata habari.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kupata habari.

sections.title: Freedom of association
sections.simplified: Right to form and join groups.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uhuru wa kujumuika. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has the right to freedom of association, which includes the right to form, join or participate in the activities of an association of any kind.
(2) A person shall not be compelled to join an association of any kind.
(3) Any legislation that requires registration of an association of any kind shall provide that— (a) registration may not be withheld or withdrawn unreasonably; and
(b) there shall be a right to have a fair hearing before a registration is cancelled.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to freedom of association, which includes the right to form, join or participate in the activities of an association of any kind. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Freedom of association, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You must not be compelled to join an association of any kind. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Freedom of association, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uhuru wa kujumuika.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uhuru wa kujumuika.

sections.title: Assembly, demonstration, picketing and petition
sections.simplified: Peaceful assembly.
sections.swSimplified: Peaceful assembly.
sections.originalText: Every person has the right, peaceably and unarmed, to assemble, to demonstrate, to picket, and to present petitions to public authorities.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Kamau has the right, peaceably and unarmed, to assemble, to demonstrate, to picket, and to present petitions to public authorities. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Assembly, demonstration, picketing and petition, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: Assembly, demonstration, picketing and petition. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Assembly, demonstration, picketing and petition, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu mikusanyiko, maandamano, piketi na malalamiko.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu mikusanyiko, maandamano, piketi na malalamiko.

sections.title: Political rights
sections.simplified: Right to vote.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu haki za kisiasa. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every citizen is free to make political choices, which includes the right— (a) to form, or participate in forming, a political party;
(b) to participate in the activities of, or recruit members for, a political party; or
(c) to campaign for a political party or cause.

(2) Every citizen has the right to free, fair and regular elections based on universal suffrage and the free expression of the will of the electors for— (a) any elective public body or office established under this Constitution; or
(b) any office of any political party of which the citizen is a member.

(3) Every adult citizen has the right, without unreasonable restrictions— (a) to be registered as a voter;
(b) to vote by secret ballot in any election or referendum; and
(c) to be a candidate for public office, or office within a political party of which the citizen is a member and, if elected, to hold office.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Every citizen is free to make political choices, which includes the right— (a) to form, or participate in forming, a political party; (b) to participate in the activities of, or recruit members for, a political party; or (c) to campaign for a political party or cause. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Political rights, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Every citizen has the right to free, fair and regular elections based on universal suffrage and the free expression of the will of the electors for— (a) any elective public body or office established under this Constitution; or (b) any office of any political party of which the citizen is a member. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Political rights, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu haki za kisiasa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu haki za kisiasa.

sections.title: Freedom of movement and residence
sections.simplified: Move and live anywhere in Kenya.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uhuru wa kutembea na kuishi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has the right to freedom of movement.
(2) Every person has the right to leave Kenya.
(3) Every citizen has the right to enter, remain in and reside anywhere in Kenya.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to freedom of movement. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Freedom of movement and residence, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You has the right to leave Kenya. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Freedom of movement and residence, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uhuru wa kutembea na kuishi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uhuru wa kutembea na kuishi.

sections.title: Protection of right to property
sections.simplified: Acquire and own land and property.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu protection of haki to mali. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Subject to Article 65, every person has the right, either individually or in association with others, to acquire and own property— (a) of any description; and
(b) in any part of Kenya.

(2) Parliament shall not enact a law that permits the State or any person— (a) to arbitrarily deprive a person of property of any description or of any interest in, or right over, any property of any description; or
(b) to limit, or in any way restrict the enjoyment of any right under this Article on the basis of any of the grounds specified or contemplated in Article 27(4).

(3) The State shall not deprive a person of property of any description, or of any interest in, or right over, property of any description, unless the deprivation— (a) results from an acquisition of land or an interest in land or a conversion of an interest in land, or title to land, in accordance with Chapter Five; or
(b) is for a public purpose or in the public interest and is carried out in accordance with this Constitution and any Act of Parliament that— (i) requires prompt payment in full, of just compensation to the person; and
(ii) allows any person who has an interest in, or right over, that property a right of access to a court of law.

(4) Provision may be made for compensation to be paid to occupants in good faith of land acquired under clause (3) who may not hold title to the land.
(5) The State shall support, promote and protect the intellectual property rights of the people of Kenya.
(6) The rights under this Article do not extend to any property that has been found to have been unlawfully acquired.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Subject to Article 65, You has the right, either individually or in association with others, to acquire and own property— (a) of any description; and (b) in any part of Kenya. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Protection of right to property, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament must not enact a law that permits the State or You— (a) to arbitrarily deprive You of property of any description or of any interest in, or right over, any property of any description; or (b) to limit, or in any way restrict the enjoyment of any right under this Article on the basis of any of the grounds specified or contemplated in Article 27(4). That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Protection of right to property, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Mtu akitaka kuchukua ardhi, nyumba, au mali yako bila haki, ibara hii husaidia kueleza ulinzi wako.
Kukiwa na mgogoro wa ardhi ya familia, kufukuzwa, au shida ya ardhi ya jamii, ibara hii inaweza kuwa muhimu.

sections.title: Labour relations
sections.simplified: Fair labor practices and trade unions.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu mahusiano ya kazi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has the right to fair labour practices.
(2) Every worker has the right— (a) to fair remuneration;
(b) to reasonable working conditions;
(c) to form, join or participate in the activities and programmes of a trade union; and
(d) to go on strike.

(3) Every employer has the right— (a) to form and join an employers' organisation; and
(b) to participate in the activities and programmes of an employers organisation.

(4) Every trade union and every employers’ organisation has the right— (a) to determine its own administration, programmes and activities;
(b) to organise; and
(c) to form and join a federation.

(5) Every trade union, employers’ organisation and employer has the right to engage in collective bargaining.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to fair labour practices. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Labour relations, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Every worker has the right— (a) to fair remuneration; (b) to reasonable working conditions; (c) to form, join or participate in the activities and programmes of a trade union; and (d) to go on strike. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Labour relations, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu mahusiano ya kazi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu mahusiano ya kazi.

sections.title: Environment
sections.simplified: Clean and healthy environment.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu environment. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
Every person has the right to a clean and healthy environment, which includes the right— (a) to have the environment protected for the benefit of present and future generations through legislative and other measures, particularly those contemplated in Article 69; and
(b) to have obligations relating to the environment fulfilled under Article 70.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Kamau has the right to a clean and healthy environment, which includes the right— (a) to have the environment protected for the benefit of present and future generations through legislative and other measures, particularly those contemplated in Article 69; and (b) to have obligations relating to the environment fulfilled under Article 70. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Environment, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Environment. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Environment, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu environment.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu environment.

sections.title: Economic and social rights
sections.simplified: Health, housing, food, water, education.
sections.swSimplified: Health, makazi, chakula, water, education.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right— (a) to the highest attainable standard of health, which includes the right to health care services, including reproductive health care; (b) to accessible and adequate housing, and to reasonable standards of sanitation; (c) to be free from hunger, and to have adequate food of acceptable quality; (d) to clean and safe water in adequate quantities; (e) to social security; and (f) to education. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Economic and social rights, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You must not be denied emergency medical treatment. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Economic and social rights, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ibara hii.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ibara hii.

sections.title: Language and culture
sections.simplified: Use your language and culture.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has the right to use the language, and to participate in the cultural life, of the person’s choice.
(2) A person belonging to a cultural or linguistic community has the right, with other members of that community— (a) to enjoy the person’s culture and use the person’s language; or
(b) to form, join and maintain cultural and linguistic associations and other organs of civil society.

(3) A person shall not compel another person to perform, observe or undergo any cultural practice or rite.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to use the language, and to participate in the cultural life, of the person’s choice. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Language and culture, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You belonging to a cultural or linguistic community has the right, with other members of that community— (a) to enjoy the person’s culture and use the person’s language; or (b) to form, join and maintain cultural and linguistic associations and other organs of civil society. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Language and culture, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ibara hii.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ibara hii.

sections.title: Family
sections.simplified: Family is the fundamental unit of society.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) The family is the natural and fundamental unit of society and the necessary basis of social order, and shall enjoy the recognition and protection of the State.
(2) Every adult has the right to marry a person of the opposite sex, based on the free consent of the parties.
(3) Parties to a marriage are entitled to equal rights at the time of the marriage, during the marriage and at the dissolution of the marriage.
(4) Parliament shall enact legislation that recognises— (a) marriages concluded under any tradition, or system of religious, personal or family law; and
(b) any system of personal and family law under any tradition, or adhered to by persons professing a particular religion,
to the extent that any such marriages or systems of law are consistent with this Constitution.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The family is the natural and fundamental unit of society and the necessary basis of social order, and must enjoy the recognition and protection of the State. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Family, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Every adult has the right to marry You of the opposite sex, based on the free consent of the parties. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Family, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ibara hii.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ibara hii.

sections.title: Consumer rights
sections.simplified: Protection from poor goods and services.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu haki za watumiaji. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Consumers have the right— (a) to goods and services of reasonable quality; (b) to the information necessary for them to gain full benefit from goods and services; (c) to the protection of your health, safety, and economic interests; and (d) to compensation for loss or injury arising from defects in goods or services. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Consumer rights, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament must enact legislation to provide for consumer protection and for fair, honest and decent advertising. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Consumer rights, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu haki za watumiaji.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu haki za watumiaji.

sections.title: Fair administrative action
sections.simplified: Efficient and lawful government service.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu hatua ya kiutawala ya haki. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has the right to administrative action that is expeditious, efficient, lawful, reasonable and procedurally fair.
(2) If a right or fundamental freedom of a person has been or is likely to be adversely affected by administrative action, the person has the right to be given written reasons for the action.
(3) Parliament shall enact legislation to give effect to the rights in clause (1) and that legislation shall— (a) provide for the review of administrative action by a court or, if appropriate, an independent and impartial tribunal; and
(b) promote efficient administration.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to administrative action that is expeditious, efficient, lawful, reasonable and procedurally fair. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Fair administrative action, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) If a right or fundamental freedom of You has been or is likely to be adversely affected by administrative action, the person has the right to be given written reasons for the action. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Fair administrative action, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu hatua ya kiutawala ya haki.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu hatua ya kiutawala ya haki.

exampleSources.title: Teachers Service Commission Corrects Personal Information
The Fair Administrative Action Guide Launch

sections.title: Access to justice
sections.simplified: Access to justice for all.
sections.swSimplified: Access to haki for all.
sections.originalText: The State shall ensure access to justice for all persons and, if any fee is required, it shall be reasonable and shall not impede access to justice.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: the State must ensure access to justice for all persons and, if any fee is required, it must be reasonable and must not impede access to justice. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Access to justice, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Access to justice. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Access to justice, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Kesi yako ikicheleweshwa, kufichwa, au kushughulikiwa bila haki, ibara hii husaidia kueleza mchakato wa haki unapaswa kufanana na nini.
Mtu akipelekwa mahakamani, anapaswa kusikilizwa vizuri. Ibara hii husaidia kueleza wazo hilo la msingi.

exampleSources.title: The Fair Administrative Action Guide Launch

sections.title: Rights of arrested persons
sections.simplified: Right to remain silent and see a lawyer.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu haki za waliokamatwa. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) An arrested person has the right— (a) to be informed promptly, in a language that the person understands, of— (i) the reason for the arrest; (ii) the right to remain silent; and (iii) the consequences of not remaining silent; (b) to remain silent; (c) to communicate with an advocate, and other persons whose assistance is necessary; (d) not to be compelled to make any confession or admission that could be used in evidence against the person; (e) to be held separately from persons who are serving a sentence; (f) to be brought before a court as soon as reasonably possible, but not later than— (i) twenty-four hours after being arrested; or (ii) if the twenty-four hours ends outside ordinary court hours, or on a day that is not an ordinary court day, the end of the next court day; (g) at the first court appearance, to be charged or informed of the reason for the detention continuing, or to be released; and (h) to be released on bond or bail, on reasonable conditions, pending a charge or trial, unless there are compelling reasons not to be released. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Rights of arrested persons, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You must not be remanded in custody for an offence if the offence is punishable by a fine only or by imprisonment for not more than six months. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Rights of arrested persons, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu haki za waliokamatwa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu haki za waliokamatwa.

exampleSources.title: Ways To Raise Complaints On Policing

sections.title: Fair hearing
sections.simplified: Fair and public trial.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu fair hearing. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has the right to have any dispute that can be resolved by the application of law decided in a fair and public hearing before a court or, if appropriate, another independent and impartial tribunal or body. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Fair hearing, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Every accused person has the right to a fair trial, which includes the right— (a) to be presumed innocent until the contrary is proved; (b) to be informed of the charge, with sufficient detail to answer it; (c) to have adequate time and facilities to prepare a defence; (d) to a public trial before a court established under this Constitution; (e) to have the trial begin and conclude without unreasonable delay; (f) to be present when being tried, unless the conduct of the accused person makes it impossible for the trial to proceed; (g) to choose, and be represented by, an advocate, and to be informed of this right promptly; (h) to have an advocate assigned to the accused person by the State and at State expense, if substantial injustice would otherwise result, and to be informed of this right promptly; (i) to remain silent, and not to testify during the proceedings; (j) to be informed in advance of the evidence the prosecution intends to rely on, and to have reasonable access to that evidence; (k) to adduce and challenge evidence; (l) to refuse to give self-incriminating evidence; (m) to have the assistance of an interpreter without payment if the accused person cannot understand the language used at the trial; (n) not to be convicted for an act or omission that at the time it was committed or omitted was not— (i) an offence in Kenya; or (ii) a crime under international law; (o) not to be tried for an offence in respect of an act or omission for which the accused person has previously been either acquitted or convicted; (p) to the benefit of the least severe of the prescribed punishments for an offence, if the prescribed punishment for the offence has been changed between the time that the offence was committed and the time of sentencing; and (q) if convicted, to appeal to, or apply for review by, a higher court as prescribed by law. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Fair hearing, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Kesi yako ikienda mahakamani, ibara hii husaidia kueleza kusikilizwa kwa haki kunapaswa kufanana na nini.
Mtu akihukumiwa bila kusikilizwa vizuri, ibara hii husaidia kueleza kwa nini hilo si haki.
