sections.title: Rights of persons detained, held in custody or imprisoned
sections.simplified: Rights while imprisoned.
sections.swSimplified: Haki while imprisoned.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person who is detained, held in custody or imprisoned under the law, retains all the rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights, except to the extent that any particular right or a fundamental freedom is clearly incompatible with the fact that the person is detained, held in custody or imprisoned.
(2) A person who is detained or held in custody is entitled to petition for an order of habeas corpus.
(3) Parliament shall enact legislation that— (a) provides for the humane treatment of persons detained, held in custody or imprisoned; and
(b) takes into account the relevant international human rights instruments.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You who is detained, held in custody or imprisoned under the law, retains all the rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights, except to the extent that any particular right or a fundamental freedom is clearly incompatible with the fact that the person is detained, held in custody or imprisoned. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Rights of persons detained, held in custody or imprisoned, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You who is detained or held in custody is entitled to petition for an order of habeas corpus. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Rights of persons detained, held in custody or imprisoned, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu haki za waliozuiliwa au kufungwa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu haki za waliozuiliwa au kufungwa.

exampleSources.title: Ways To Raise Complaints On Policing

sections.title: Interpretation of this Part
sections.simplified: (1) This Part elaborates certain rights to ensure greater certainty as to the application of those rights and fundamental freedoms to certain groups of persons. (2) This Part must not be construed as limiting or qualifying any right.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) This Part elaborates certain rights to ensure greater certainty as to the application of those rights and fundamental freedoms to certain groups of persons.
(2) This Part shall not be construed as limiting or qualifying any right.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) This Part elaborates certain rights to ensure greater certainty as to the application of those rights and fundamental freedoms to certain groups of persons. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Interpretation of this Part, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) This Part must not be construed as limiting or qualifying any right. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Interpretation of this Part, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ibara hii.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ibara hii.

sections.title: Children
sections.simplified: Basic education and shelter for children.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu watoto. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Every child has the right— (a) to a name and nationality from birth; (b) to free and compulsory basic education; (c) to basic nutrition, shelter and health care; (d) to be protected from abuse, neglect, harmful cultural practices, all forms of violence, inhuman treatment and punishment, and hazardous or exploitative labour; (e) to parental care and protection, which includes equal responsibility of the mother and father to provide for the child, whether they are married to each other or not; and (f) not to be detained, except as a measure of last resort, and when detained, to be held— (i) for the shortest appropriate period of time; and (ii) separate from adults and in conditions that take account of the child’s sex and age. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Children, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) A child’s best interests are of paramount importance in every matter concerning the child. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Children, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Mtoto akinyimwa shule, chakula, makazi, au usalama, ibara hii husaidia kueleza haki za mtoto.
Watu wazima wakifanya uamuzi unaomdhuru mtoto, ibara hii hutukumbusha kuwa mtoto anapaswa kupewa nafasi ya kwanza.

sections.title: Persons with disabilities
sections.simplified: Dignity and access for people with disabilities.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu watu wenye ulemavu. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You with any disability is entitled— (a) to be treated with dignity and respect and to be addressed and referred to in a manner that is not demeaning; (b) to access educational institutions and facilities for persons with disabilities that are integrated into society to the extent compatible with the interests of the person; (c) to reasonable access to all places, public transport and information; (d) to use Sign language, Braille or other appropriate means of communication; and (e) to access materials and devices to overcome constraints arising from the person’s disability. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Persons with disabilities, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) the State must ensure the progressive implementation of the principle that at least five percent of the members of the public in elective and appointive bodies are persons with disabilities. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Persons with disabilities, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu watu wenye ulemavu.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu watu wenye ulemavu.

sections.title: Youth
sections.simplified: Jobs and education for the youth.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: the State must take measures, including affirmative action programmes, to ensure that the youth— (a) access relevant education and training; (b) have opportunities to associate, be represented and participate in political, social, economic and other spheres of life; (c) access employment; and (d) are protected from harmful cultural practices and exploitation. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Youth, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Youth. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Youth, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: State of emergency
sections.simplified: (1) A state of emergency can be declared only under Article 132 (4) (d) and only when— (a) the State is threatened by war, invasion, general insurrection, disorder, natural disaster or other public emergency; and (b) the declaration is necessary to meet the circumstances for which the emergency is declared. (2) A declaration of a state of emergency, and any legislation enacted or other action taken in consequence of the declaration, must be effective only— (a) prospectively; and (b) for not longer than fourteen days from the date of the declaration, unless the National Assembly resolves to extend the declaration. (3) The National Assembly can extend a declaration of a state of emergency— (a) by resolution adopted— (i) following a public debate in the National Assembly; and (ii) by the majorities specified in clause (4); and (b) for not longer than two months at a time.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu hali ya hatari. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A state of emergency may be declared only under Article 132 (4) (d) and only when— (a) the State is threatened by war, invasion, general insurrection, disorder, natural disaster or other public emergency; and
(b) the declaration is necessary to meet the circumstances for which the emergency is declared.

(2) A declaration of a state of emergency, and any legislation enacted or other action taken in consequence of the declaration, shall be effective only— (a) prospectively; and
(b) for not longer than fourteen days from the date of the declaration, unless the National Assembly resolves to extend the declaration.

(3) The National Assembly may extend a declaration of a state of emergency— (a) by resolution adopted— (i) following a public debate in the National Assembly; and
(ii) by the majorities specified in clause (4); and

(b) for not longer than two months at a time.

(4) The first extension of the declaration of a state of emergency requires a supporting vote of at least two-thirds of all the members of the National Assembly, and any subsequent extension requires a supporting vote of at least three-quarters of all the members of the National Assembly.
(5) The Supreme Court may decide on the validity of— (a) a declaration of a state of emergency;
(b) any extension of a declaration of a state of emergency; and
(c) any legislation enacted, or other action taken, in consequence of a declaration of a state of emergency.

(6) Any legislation enacted in consequence of a declaration of a state of emergency— (a) may limit a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights only to the extent that— (i) the limitation is strictly required by the emergency; and
(ii) the legislation is consistent with the Republic’s obligations under international law applicable to a state of emergency; and

(b) shall not take effect until it is published in the Gazette.

(7) A declaration of a state of emergency, or legislation enacted or other action taken in consequence of any declaration, may not permit or authorise the indemnification of the State, or of any person, in respect of any unlawful act or omission.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) A state of emergency can be declared only under Article 132 (4) (d) and only when— (a) the State is threatened by war, invasion, general insurrection, disorder, natural disaster or other public emergency; and (b) the declaration is necessary to meet the circumstances for which the emergency is declared. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on State of emergency, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) A declaration of a state of emergency, and any legislation enacted or other action taken in consequence of the declaration, must be effective only— (a) prospectively; and (b) for not longer than fourteen days from the date of the declaration, unless the National Assembly resolves to extend the declaration. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on State of emergency, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu hali ya hatari.

sections.title: Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission
sections.swTitle: Tume ya Kitaifa ya Haki za Kibinadamu na Usawa Kenya
sections.simplified: (1) There is established the Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission. (2) The functions of the Commission are— (a) to promote respect for human rights and develop a culture of human rights in the Republic; (b) to promote gender equality and equity generally and to coordinate and facilitate gender mainstreaming in national development; (c) to promote the protection, and observance of human rights in public and private institutions; (d) to monitor, investigate and report on the observance of human rights in all spheres of life in the Republic, including observance by the national security organs; (e) to receive and investigate complaints about alleged abuses of human rights and take steps to secure appropriate redress where human rights have been violated; (f) on its own initiative or on the basis of complaints, to investigate or research a matter in respect of human rights, and make recommendations to improve the functioning of State organs; (g) to act as the principal organ of the State in ensuring compliance with obligations under treaties and conventions relating to human rights; (h) to investigate any conduct in state affairs, or any act or omission in public administration in any sphere of government, that is alleged or suspected to be prejudicial or improper or to result in any impropriety or prejudice; (i) to investigate complaints of abuse of power, unfair treatment, manifest injustice or unlawful, oppressive, unfair or unresponsive official conduct; (j) to report on complaints investigated under paragraphs (h) and (i) and take remedial action; and (k) to perform any other functions prescribed by legislation. (3) You has the right to complain to the Commission, alleging that a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights has been denied, violated or infringed, or is threatened.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu tume ya Kitaifa ya Haki za Kibinadamu na Usawa Kenya. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) There is established the Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The functions of the Commission are— (a) to promote respect for human rights and develop a culture of human rights in the Republic; (b) to promote gender equality and equity generally and to coordinate and facilitate gender mainstreaming in national development; (c) to promote the protection, and observance of human rights in public and private institutions; (d) to monitor, investigate and report on the observance of human rights in all spheres of life in the Republic, including observance by the national security organs; (e) to receive and investigate complaints about alleged abuses of human rights and take steps to secure appropriate redress where human rights have been violated; (f) on its own initiative or on the basis of complaints, to investigate or research a matter in respect of human rights, and make recommendations to improve the functioning of State organs; (g) to act as the principal organ of the State in ensuring compliance with obligations under treaties and conventions relating to human rights; (h) to investigate any conduct in state affairs, or any act or omission in public administration in any sphere of government, that is alleged or suspected to be prejudicial or improper or to result in any impropriety or prejudice; (i) to investigate complaints of abuse of power, unfair treatment, manifest injustice or unlawful, oppressive, unfair or unresponsive official conduct; (j) to report on complaints investigated under paragraphs (h) and (i) and take remedial action; and (k) to perform any other functions prescribed by legislation. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Principles of land policy
sections.simplified: Equitable land use.
sections.swSimplified: Equitable ardhi kutumia.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Land in Kenya must be held, used and managed in a manner that is equitable, efficient, productive and sustainable, and in accordance with the following principles— (a) equitable access to land; (b) security of land rights; (c) sustainable and productive management of land resources; (d) transparent and cost effective administration of land; (e) sound conservation and protection of ecologically sensitive areas; (f) elimination of gender discrimination in law, customs and practices related to land and property in land; and (g) encouragement of communities to settle land disputes through recognised local community initiatives consistent with this Constitution. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Principles of land policy, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) These principles must be implemented through a national land policy developed and reviewed regularly by the national government and through legislation. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Principles of land policy, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kanuni za sera ya ardhi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kanuni za sera ya ardhi.

sections.title: Classification of land
sections.simplified: Public/Community/Private.
sections.swSimplified: Public/Community/Private.
sections.originalText:
(1) All land in Kenya belongs to the people of Kenya collectively as a nation, as communities and as individuals.
(2) Land in Kenya is classified as public, community or private.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) All land in Kenya belongs to the people of Kenya collectively as a nation, as communities and as individuals. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Classification of land, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Land in Kenya is classified as public, community or private. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Classification of land, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu classification of ardhi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu classification of ardhi.

sections.title: Public land
sections.simplified: (1) Public land is— (a) land which at the effective date was unalienated government land as defined by an Act of Parliament in force at the effective date; (b) land lawfully held, used or occupied by any State organ, except any such land that is occupied by the State organ as lessee under a private lease; (c) land transferred to the State by way of sale, reversion or surrender; (d) land in respect of which no individual or community ownership can be established by any legal process; (e) land in respect of which no heir can be identified by any legal process; (f) all minerals and mineral oils as defined by law; (g) government forests other than forests to which Article 63(2)(d)(i) applies, government game reserves, water catchment areas, national parks, government animal sanctuaries, and specially protected areas; (h) all roads and thoroughfares provided for by an Act of Parliament; (i) all rivers, lakes and other water bodies as defined by an Act of Parliament; (j) the territorial sea, the exclusive economic zone and the sea bed; (k) the continental shelf; (l) all land between the high and low water marks; (m) any land not classified as private or community land under this Constitution; and (n) any other land declared to be public land by an Act of Parliament— (i) in force at the effective date; or (ii) enacted after the effective date. (2) Public land must vest in and be held by a county government in trust for the people resident in the county, and must be administered on your behalf by the National Land Commission, if it is classified under— (a) clause (1)(a), (c), (d) or (e); and (b) clause (1)(b), other than land held, used or occupied by a national State organ. (3) Public land classified under clause (1) (f) to (m) must vest in and be held by the national government in trust for the people of Kenya and must be administered on your behalf by the National Land Commission.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu public ardhi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Public land is— (a) land which at the effective date was unalienated government land as defined by an Act of Parliament in force at the effective date; (b) land lawfully held, used or occupied by any State organ, except any such land that is occupied by the State organ as lessee under a private lease; (c) land transferred to the State by way of sale, reversion or surrender; (d) land in respect of which no individual or community ownership can be established by any legal process; (e) land in respect of which no heir can be identified by any legal process; (f) all minerals and mineral oils as defined by law; (g) government forests other than forests to which Article 63(2)(d)(i) applies, government game reserves, water catchment areas, national parks, government animal sanctuaries, and specially protected areas; (h) all roads and thoroughfares provided for by an Act of Parliament; (i) all rivers, lakes and other water bodies as defined by an Act of Parliament; (j) the territorial sea, the exclusive economic zone and the sea bed; (k) the continental shelf; (l) all land between the high and low water marks; (m) any land not classified as private or community land under this Constitution; and (n) any other land declared to be public land by an Act of Parliament— (i) in force at the effective date; or (ii) enacted after the effective date. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Public land, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Public land must vest in and be held by a county government in trust for the people resident in the county, and must be administered on your behalf by the National Land Commission, if it is classified under— (a) clause (1)(a), (c), (d) or (e); and (b) clause (1)(b), other than land held, used or occupied by a national State organ. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Public land, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu public ardhi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu public ardhi.

sections.title: Community land
sections.simplified: (1) Community land must vest in and be held by communities identified on the basis of ethnicity, culture or similar community of interest. (2) Community land consists of— (a) land lawfully registered in the name of group representatives under the provisions of any law; (b) land lawfully transferred to a specific community by any process of law; (c) any other land declared to be community land by an Act of Parliament; and (d) land that is— (i) lawfully held, managed or used by specific communities as community forests, grazing areas or shrines; (ii) ancestral lands and lands traditionally occupied by hunter-gatherer communities; or (iii) lawfully held as trust land by the county governments, but not including any public land held in trust by the county government under Article 62(2). (3) Any unregistered community land must be held in trust by county governments on behalf of the communities for which it is held.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ardhi ya jamii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Community land shall vest in and be held by communities identified on the basis of ethnicity, culture or similar community of interest.
(2) Community land consists of— (a) land lawfully registered in the name of group representatives under the provisions of any law;
(b) land lawfully transferred to a specific community by any process of law;
(c) any other land declared to be community land by an Act of Parliament; and
(d) land that is— (i) lawfully held, managed or used by specific communities as community forests, grazing areas or shrines;
(ii) ancestral lands and lands traditionally occupied by hunter-gatherer communities; or
(iii) lawfully held as trust land by the county governments,

but not including any public land held in trust by the county government under Article 62(2).
(3) Any unregistered community land shall be held in trust by county governments on behalf of the communities for which it is held.
(4) Community land shall not be disposed of or otherwise used except in terms of legislation specifying the nature and extent of the rights of members of each community individually and collectively.
(5) Parliament shall enact legislation to give effect to this Article.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Community land must vest in and be held by communities identified on the basis of ethnicity, culture or similar community of interest. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Community land, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Community land consists of— (a) land lawfully registered in the name of group representatives under the provisions of any law; (b) land lawfully transferred to a specific community by any process of law; (c) any other land declared to be community land by an Act of Parliament; and (d) land that is— (i) lawfully held, managed or used by specific communities as community forests, grazing areas or shrines; (ii) ancestral lands and lands traditionally occupied by hunter-gatherer communities; or (iii) lawfully held as trust land by the county governments, but not including any public land held in trust by the county government under Article 62(2). That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Community land, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ardhi ya jamii.

sections.title: Private land
sections.simplified: Private land consists of — (a) registered land held by You under any freehold tenure; (b) land held by You under leasehold tenure; and (c) any other land declared private land under an Act of Parliament. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ardhi ya kibinafsi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Private land consists of — (a) registered land held by You under any freehold tenure; (b) land held by You under leasehold tenure; and (c) any other land declared private land under an Act of Parliament. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Private land, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Private land. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Private land, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ardhi ya kibinafsi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ardhi ya kibinafsi.

sections.title: Landholding by non-citizens
sections.simplified: (1) You who is not a citizen can hold land on the basis of leasehold tenure only, and any such lease, however granted, must not exceed ninety-nine years. (2) If a provision of any agreement, deed, conveyance or document of whatever nature purports to confer on You who is not a citizen an interest in land greater than a ninety-nine year lease, the provision must be regarded as conferring on the person a ninety-nine year leasehold interest, and no more. (3) For purposes of this Article— (a) a body corporate must be regarded as a citizen only if the body corporate is wholly owned by one or more citizens; and (b) property held in trust must be regarded as being held by a citizen only if all of the beneficial interest of the trust is held by persons who are citizens.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu landholding by non-raia. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person who is not a citizen may hold land on the basis of leasehold tenure only, and any such lease, however granted, shall not exceed ninety-nine years.
(2) If a provision of any agreement, deed, conveyance or document of whatever nature purports to confer on a person who is not a citizen an interest in land greater than a ninety-nine year lease, the provision shall be regarded as conferring on the person a ninety-nine year leasehold interest, and no more.
(3) For purposes of this Article— (a) a body corporate shall be regarded as a citizen only if the body corporate is wholly owned by one or more citizens; and
(b) property held in trust shall be regarded as being held by a citizen only if all of the beneficial interest of the trust is held by persons who are citizens.

(4) Parliament may enact legislation to make further provision for the operation of this Article.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You who is not a citizen can hold land on the basis of leasehold tenure only, and any such lease, however granted, must not exceed ninety-nine years. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Landholding by non-citizens, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) If a provision of any agreement, deed, conveyance or document of whatever nature purports to confer on You who is not a citizen an interest in land greater than a ninety-nine year lease, the provision must be regarded as conferring on the person a ninety-nine year leasehold interest, and no more. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Landholding by non-citizens, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Regulation of land use and property
sections.swTitle: Udhibiti wa matumizi ya ardhi na mali
sections.simplified: (1) the State can regulate the use of any land, or any interest in or right over any land, in the interest of defence, public safety, public order, public morality, public health, or land use planning. (2) Parliament must enact legislation ensuring that investments in property benefit local communities and your economies.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu udhibiti wa matumizi ya ardhi na mali. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) The State may regulate the use of any land, or any interest in or right over any land, in the interest of defence, public safety, public order, public morality, public health, or land use planning.
(2) Parliament shall enact legislation ensuring that investments in property benefit local communities and their economies.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) the State can regulate the use of any land, or any interest in or right over any land, in the interest of defence, public safety, public order, public morality, public health, or land use planning. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Regulation of land use and property, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament must enact legislation ensuring that investments in property benefit local communities and your economies. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Regulation of land use and property, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: National Land Commission
sections.simplified: (1) There is established the National Land Commission. (2) The functions of the National Land Commission are— (a) to manage public land on behalf of the national and county governments; (b) to recommend a national land policy to the national government; (c) to advise the national government on a comprehensive programme for the registration of title in land throughout Kenya; (d) to conduct research related to land and the use of natural resources, and make recommendations to appropriate authorities; (e) to initiate investigations, on its own initiative or on a complaint, into present or historical land injustices, and recommend appropriate redress; (f) to encourage the application of traditional dispute resolution mechanisms in land conflicts; (g) to assess tax on land and premiums on immovable property in any area designated by law; and (h) to monitor and have oversight responsibilities over land use planning throughout the country. (3) The National Land Commission can perform any other functions prescribed by national legislation.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu national Land Commission. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) There is established the National Land Commission. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on National Land Commission, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The functions of the National Land Commission are— (a) to manage public land on behalf of the national and county governments; (b) to recommend a national land policy to the national government; (c) to advise the national government on a comprehensive programme for the registration of title in land throughout Kenya; (d) to conduct research related to land and the use of natural resources, and make recommendations to appropriate authorities; (e) to initiate investigations, on its own initiative or on a complaint, into present or historical land injustices, and recommend appropriate redress; (f) to encourage the application of traditional dispute resolution mechanisms in land conflicts; (g) to assess tax on land and premiums on immovable property in any area designated by law; and (h) to monitor and have oversight responsibilities over land use planning throughout the country. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on National Land Commission, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu national Land Commission.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu national Land Commission.

sections.title: Legislation on land
sections.simplified: Parliament must— (a) revise, consolidate and rationalise existing land laws; (b) revise sectoral land use laws in accordance with the principles set out in Article 60(1); and (c) enact legislation— (i) to prescribe minimum and maximum land holding acreages in respect of private land; (ii) to regulate the manner in which any land can be converted from one category to another; (iii) to regulate the recognition and protection of matrimonial property and in particular the matrimonial home during and on the termination of marriage; (iv) to protect, conserve and provide access to all public land; (v) to enable the review of all grants or dispositions of public land to establish your propriety or legality; (vi) to protect the dependants of deceased persons holding interests in any land, including the interests of spouses in actual occupation of land; and (vii) to provide for any other matter necessary to give effect to the provisions of this Chapter. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu sheria kuhusu ardhi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Parliament must— (a) revise, consolidate and rationalise existing land laws; (b) revise sectoral land use laws in accordance with the principles set out in Article 60(1); and (c) enact legislation— (i) to prescribe minimum and maximum land holding acreages in respect of private land; (ii) to regulate the manner in which any land can be converted from one category to another; (iii) to regulate the recognition and protection of matrimonial property and in particular the matrimonial home during and on the termination of marriage; (iv) to protect, conserve and provide access to all public land; (v) to enable the review of all grants or dispositions of public land to establish your propriety or legality; (vi) to protect the dependants of deceased persons holding interests in any land, including the interests of spouses in actual occupation of land; and (vii) to provide for any other matter necessary to give effect to the provisions of this Chapter. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on land, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Legislation on land. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on land, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu sheria kuhusu ardhi.

sections.title: Obligations in respect of the environment
sections.simplified: Protect biodiversity.
sections.swSimplified: Linda biodiversity.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) the State must— (a) ensure sustainable exploitation, utilisation, management and conservation of the environment and natural resources, and ensure the equitable sharing of the accruing benefits; (b) work to achieve and maintain a tree cover of at least ten per cent of the land area of Kenya; (c) protect and enhance intellectual property in, and indigenous knowledge of, biodiversity and the genetic resources of the communities; (d) encourage public participation in the management, protection and conservation of the environment; (e) protect genetic resources and biological diversity; (f) establish systems of environmental impact assessment, environmental audit and monitoring of the environment; (g) eliminate processes and activities that are likely to endanger the environment; and (h) utilise the environment and natural resources for the benefit of the people of Kenya. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Obligations in respect of the environment, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You has a duty to cooperate with State organs and other persons to protect and conserve the environment and ensure ecologically sustainable development and use of natural resources. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Obligations in respect of the environment, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu wajibu kuhusu mazingira.

sections.title: Enforcement of environmental rights
sections.simplified: (1) If You alleges that a right to a clean and healthy environment recognised and protected under Article 42 has been, is being or is likely to be, denied, violated, infringed or threatened, the person can apply to a court for redress in addition to any other legal remedies that are available in respect to the same matter. (2) On application under clause (1), the court can make any order, or give any directions, it considers appropriate— (a) to prevent, stop or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment; (b) to compel any public officer to take measures to prevent or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment; or (c) to provide compensation for any victim of a violation of the right to a clean and healthy environment. (3) For the purposes of this Article, an applicant does not have to demonstrate that You has incurred loss or suffered injury.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu utekelezaji wa haki za mazingira. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) If a person alleges that a right to a clean and healthy environment recognised and protected under Article 42 has been, is being or is likely to be, denied, violated, infringed or threatened, the person may apply to a court for redress in addition to any other legal remedies that are available in respect to the same matter.
(2) On application under clause (1), the court may make any order, or give any directions, it considers appropriate— (a) to prevent, stop or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment;
(b) to compel any public officer to take measures to prevent or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment; or
(c) to provide compensation for any victim of a violation of the right to a clean and healthy environment.

(3) For the purposes of this Article, an applicant does not have to demonstrate that any person has incurred loss or suffered injury.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) If You alleges that a right to a clean and healthy environment recognised and protected under Article 42 has been, is being or is likely to be, denied, violated, infringed or threatened, the person can apply to a court for redress in addition to any other legal remedies that are available in respect to the same matter. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Enforcement of environmental rights, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) On application under clause (1), the court can make any order, or give any directions, it considers appropriate— (a) to prevent, stop or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment; (b) to compel any public officer to take measures to prevent or discontinue any act or omission that is harmful to the environment; or (c) to provide compensation for any victim of a violation of the right to a clean and healthy environment. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Enforcement of environmental rights, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu utekelezaji wa haki za mazingira.

sections.title: Agreements relating to natural resources
sections.simplified: (1) A transaction is subject to ratification by Parliament if it— (a) involves the grant of a right or concession by or on behalf of You, including the national government, to another person for the exploitation of any natural resource of Kenya; and (b) is entered into on or after the effective date. (2) Parliament must enact legislation providing for the classes of transactions subject to ratification under clause (1).
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu agreements relating to natural resources. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A transaction is subject to ratification by Parliament if it— (a) involves the grant of a right or concession by or on behalf of any person, including the national government, to another person for the exploitation of any natural resource of Kenya; and
(b) is entered into on or after the effective date.

(2) Parliament shall enact legislation providing for the classes of transactions subject to ratification under clause (1).

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Agreements relating to natural resources, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament must enact legislation providing for the classes of transactions subject to ratification under clause (1). That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Agreements relating to natural resources, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Legislation relating to the environment
sections.simplified: Parliament must enact legislation to give full effect to the provisions of this Part. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.

sections.title: Responsibilities of leadership
sections.simplified: Serve people, not self.
sections.swSimplified: Serve watu, not self.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Authority assigned to a State officer— (a) is a public trust to be exercised in a manner that— (i) is consistent with the purposes and objects of this Constitution; (ii) demonstrates respect for the people; (iii) brings honour to the nation and dignity to the office; and (iv) promotes public confidence in the integrity of the office; and (b) vests in the State officer the responsibility to serve the people, rather than the power to rule them. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Responsibilities of leadership, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The guiding principles of leadership and integrity include— (a) selection on the basis of personal integrity, competence and suitability, or election in free and fair elections; (b) objectivity and impartiality in decision making, and in ensuring that decisions are not influenced by nepotism, favouritism, other improper motives or corrupt practices; (c) selfless service based solely on the public interest, demonstrated by — (i) honesty in the execution of public duties; and (ii) the declaration of any personal interest that can conflict with public duties; (d) accountability to the public for decisions and actions; and (e) discipline and commitment in service to the people. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Responsibilities of leadership, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Oath of office of State officers
sections.swTitle: Oath of ofisi of maafisa wa Serikali
sections.simplified: Before assuming a State office, acting in a State office, or performing any functions of a State office, You must take and subscribe to the oath or affirmation of office, in the manner and form prescribed by the Third Schedule or under an Act of Parliament. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu oath of ofisi of maafisa wa Serikali. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText: Before assuming a State office, acting in a State office, or performing any functions of a State office, a person shall take and subscribe to the oath or affirmation of office, in the manner and form prescribed by the Third Schedule or under an Act of Parliament.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Before assuming a State office, acting in a State office, or performing any functions of a State office, You must take and subscribe to the oath or affirmation of office, in the manner and form prescribed by the Third Schedule or under an Act of Parliament. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Oath of office of State officers, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: Oath of office of State officers. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Oath of office of State officers, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Conduct of State officers
sections.simplified: Role model behavior.
sections.swSimplified: Role model behavior.
sections.originalText:
(1) A State officer shall behave, whether in public and official life, in private life, or in association with other persons, in a manner that avoids— (a) any conflict between personal interests and public or official duties;
(b) compromising any public or official interest in favour of a personal interest; or
(c) demeaning the office the officer holds.

(2) A person who contravenes clause (1), or Article 76, 77 or 78(2)— (a) shall be subject to the applicable disciplinary procedure for the relevant office; and
(b) may, in accordance with the disciplinary procedure referred to in paragraph (a), be dismissed or otherwise removed from office.

(3) A person who has been dismissed or otherwise removed from office for a contravention of the provisions specified in clause (2) is disqualified from holding any other State office.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) A State officer must behave, whether in public and official life, in private life, or in association with other persons, in a manner that avoids— (a) any conflict between personal interests and public or official duties; (b) compromising any public or official interest in favour of a personal interest; or (c) demeaning the office the officer holds. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Conduct of State officers, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) You who contravenes clause (1), or Article 76, 77 or 78(2)— (a) must be subject to the applicable disciplinary procedure for the relevant office; and (b) can, in accordance with the disciplinary procedure referred to in paragraph (a), be dismissed or otherwise removed from office. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Conduct of State officers, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu maadili ya maafisa wa Serikali.

sections.title: Financial probity of State officers
sections.swTitle: Uaminifu wa kifedha wa maafisa wa Serikali
sections.simplified: (1) A gift or donation to a State officer on a public or official occasion is a gift or donation to the Republic and must be delivered to the State unless exempted under an Act of Parliament. (2) A State officer must not— (a) maintain a bank account outside Kenya except in accordance with an Act of Parliament; or (b) seek or accept a personal loan or benefit in circumstances that compromise the integrity of the State officer.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uaminifu wa kifedha wa maafisa wa Serikali. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A gift or donation to a State officer on a public or official occasion is a gift or donation to the Republic and shall be delivered to the State unless exempted under an Act of Parliament.
(2) A State officer shall not— (a) maintain a bank account outside Kenya except in accordance with an Act of Parliament; or
(b) seek or accept a personal loan or benefit in circumstances that compromise the integrity of the State officer.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) A gift or donation to a State officer on a public or official occasion is a gift or donation to the Republic and must be delivered to the State unless exempted under an Act of Parliament. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Financial probity of State officers, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Restriction on activities of State officers
sections.swTitle: Vizuizi kwa shughuli za maafisa wa Serikali
sections.simplified: (1) A full-time State officer must not participate in any other gainful employment. (2) Any appointed State officer must not hold office in a political party. (3) A retired State officer who is receiving a pension from public funds must not hold more than two concurrent remunerative positions as chairperson, director or employee of— (a) a company owned or controlled by the State; or (b) a State organ.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu vizuizi kwa shughuli za maafisa wa Serikali. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A full-time State officer shall not participate in any other gainful employment.
(2) Any appointed State officer shall not hold office in a political party.
(3) A retired State officer who is receiving a pension from public funds shall not hold more than two concurrent remunerative positions as chairperson, director or employee of— (a) a company owned or controlled by the State; or
(b) a State organ.

(4) A retired State officer shall not receive remuneration from public funds other than as contemplated in clause (3).
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) A full-time State officer must not participate in any other gainful employment. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Restriction on activities of State officers, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Any appointed State officer must not hold office in a political party. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Restriction on activities of State officers, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu vizuizi kwa shughuli za maafisa wa Serikali.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu vizuizi kwa shughuli za maafisa wa Serikali.

sections.title: Citizenship and leadership
sections.simplified: (1) You is not eligible for election or appointment to a State office unless the person is a citizen of Kenya. (2) A State officer or a member of the defence forces must not hold dual citizenship. (3) Clauses (1) and (2) do not apply to— (a) judges and members of commissions; or (b) You who has been made a citizen of another country by operation of that country’s law, without ability to opt out.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uraia na uongozi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person is not eligible for election or appointment to a State office unless the person is a citizen of Kenya.
(2) A State officer or a member of the defence forces shall not hold dual citizenship.
(3) Clauses (1) and (2) do not apply to— (a) judges and members of commissions; or
(b) any person who has been made a citizen of another country by operation of that country’s law, without ability to opt out.

sections.title: Legislation to establish the ethics and anti-corruption commission
sections.swTitle: Sheria ya kuanzisha tume ya maadili na kupambana na ufisadi
sections.simplified: Parliament must enact legislation to establish an independent ethics and anti-corruption commission, which must be and have the status and powers of a commission under Chapter Fifteen, for purposes of ensuring compliance with, and enforcement of, the provisions of this Chapter. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu sheria ya kuanzisha tume ya maadili na kupambana na ufisadi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Legislation on leadership
sections.simplified: Parliament must enact legislation— (a) establishing procedures and mechanisms for the effective administration of this Chapter; (b) prescribing the penalties, in addition to the penalties referred to in Article 75, that can be imposed for a contravention of this Chapter; (c) providing for the application of this Chapter, with the necessary modifications, to public officers; and (d) making any other provision necessary for ensuring the promotion of the principles of leadership and integrity referred to in this Chapter, and the enforcement of this Chapter. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu sheria kuhusu uongozi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Parliament must enact legislation— (a) establishing procedures and mechanisms for the effective administration of this Chapter; (b) prescribing the penalties, in addition to the penalties referred to in Article 75, that can be imposed for a contravention of this Chapter; (c) providing for the application of this Chapter, with the necessary modifications, to public officers; and (d) making any other provision necessary for ensuring the promotion of the principles of leadership and integrity referred to in this Chapter, and the enforcement of this Chapter. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on leadership, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: General principles for the electoral system
sections.swTitle: Kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi
sections.simplified: Free and fair.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: The electoral system must comply with the following principles— (a) freedom of citizens to exercise your political rights under Article 38; (b) not more than two-thirds of the members of elective public bodies must be of the same gender; (c) fair representation of persons with disabilities; (d) universal suffrage based on the aspiration for fair representation and equality of vote; and (e) free and fair elections, which are— (i) by secret ballot; (ii) free from violence, intimidation, improper influence or corruption; (iii) conducted by an independent body; (iv) transparent; and (v) administered in an impartial, neutral, efficient, accurate and accountable manner. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on General principles for the electoral system, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: General principles for the electoral system. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on General principles for the electoral system, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kanuni za jumla za mfumo wa uchaguzi.

sections.title: Legislation on elections
sections.simplified: (1) Parliament must enact legislation to provide for— (a) the delimitation by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission of electoral units for election of members of the National Assembly and county assemblies; (b) the nomination of candidates; (c) the continuous registration of citizens as voters; (d) the conduct of elections and referenda and the regulation and efficient supervision of elections and referenda, including the nomination of candidates for elections; and (e) the progressive registration of citizens residing outside Kenya, and the progressive realisation of your right to vote. (2) Legislation required by clause (1)(d) must ensure that voting at every election is— (a) simple; (b) transparent; and (c) takes into account the special needs of— (i) persons with disabilities; and (ii) other persons or groups with special needs.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu sheria kuhusu uchaguzi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Parliament must enact legislation to provide for— (a) the delimitation by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission of electoral units for election of members of the National Assembly and county assemblies; (b) the nomination of candidates; (c) the continuous registration of citizens as voters; (d) the conduct of elections and referenda and the regulation and efficient supervision of elections and referenda, including the nomination of candidates for elections; and (e) the progressive registration of citizens residing outside Kenya, and the progressive realisation of your right to vote. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on elections, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Legislation required by clause (1)(d) must ensure that voting at every election is— (a) simple; (b) transparent; and (c) takes into account the special needs of— (i) persons with disabilities; and (ii) other persons or groups with special needs. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on elections, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Registration as a voter
sections.simplified: (1) You qualifies for registration as a voter at elections or referenda if the person— (a) is an adult citizen; (b) is not declared to be of unsound mind; and (c) has not been convicted of an election offence during the preceding five years. (2) A citizen who qualifies for registration as a voter must be registered at only one registration centre. (3) Administrative arrangements for the registration of voters and the conduct of elections must be designed to facilitate, and must not deny, an eligible citizen the right to vote or stand for election.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu registration as a kurar. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A person qualifies for registration as a voter at elections or referenda if the person— (a) is an adult citizen;
(b) is not declared to be of unsound mind; and
(c) has not been convicted of an election offence during the preceding five years.

(2) A citizen who qualifies for registration as a voter shall be registered at only one registration centre.
(3) Administrative arrangements for the registration of voters and the conduct of elections shall be designed to facilitate, and shall not deny, an eligible citizen the right to vote or stand for election.

sections.title: Candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct
sections.simplified: In every election, all candidates and all political parties must comply with the code of conduct prescribed by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate
sections.swTitle: Sifa za kugombea kama mgombea huru
sections.simplified: You is eligible to stand as an independent candidate for election if the person— (a) is not a member of a registered political party and has not been a member for at least three months immediately before the date of the election; and (b) satisfies the requirements of— (i) Article 99(1)(c)(i) or (ii), in the case of a candidate for election to the National Assembly or the Senate, respectively; or (ii) Article 193(1)(c)(ii), in the case of a candidate for election to a county assembly. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu sifa za kugombea kama mgombea huru. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
Any person is eligible to stand as an independent candidate for election if the person— (a) is not a member of a registered political party and has not been a member for at least three months immediately before the date of the election; and
(b) satisfies the requirements of— (i) Article 99(1)(c)(i) or (ii), in the case of a candidate for election to the National Assembly or the Senate, respectively; or
(ii) Article 193(1)(c)(ii), in the case of a candidate for election to a county assembly.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: Kamau is eligible to stand as an independent candidate for election if the person— (a) is not a member of a registered political party and has not been a member for at least three months immediately before the date of the election; and (b) satisfies the requirements of— (i) Article 99(1)(c)(i) or (ii), in the case of a candidate for election to the National Assembly or the Senate, respectively; or (ii) Article 193(1)(c)(ii), in the case of a candidate for election to a county assembly. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu sifa za kugombea kama mgombea huru.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu sifa za kugombea kama mgombea huru.

sections.title: Voting
sections.simplified: At every election, the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission must ensure that— (a) whatever voting method is used, the system is simple, accurate, verifiable, secure, accountable and transparent; (b) the votes cast are counted, tabulated and the results announced promptly by the presiding officer at each polling station; (c) the results from the polling stations are openly and accurately collated and promptly announced by the returning officer; and (d) appropriate structures and mechanisms to eliminate electoral malpractice are put in place, including the safekeeping of election materials. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kupiga kura. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: At every election, the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission must ensure that— (a) whatever voting method is used, the system is simple, accurate, verifiable, secure, accountable and transparent; (b) the votes cast are counted, tabulated and the results announced promptly by the presiding officer at each polling station; (c) the results from the polling stations are openly and accurately collated and promptly announced by the returning officer; and (d) appropriate structures and mechanisms to eliminate electoral malpractice are put in place, including the safekeeping of election materials. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Voting, that is how it should be handled.
Achieng is in a real situation: Voting. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Voting, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kupiga kura.

sections.title: Electoral disputes
sections.simplified: (1) Parliament must enact legislation to establish mechanisms for timely settling of electoral disputes. (2) Petitions concerning an election, other than a presidential election, must be filed within twenty-eight days after the declaration of the election results by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission. (3) Service of a petition can be direct or by advertisement in a newspaper with national circulation.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu migogoro ya uchaguzi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Parliament shall enact legislation to establish mechanisms for timely settling of electoral disputes.
(2) Petitions concerning an election, other than a presidential election, shall be filed within twenty-eight days after the declaration of the election results by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission.
(3) Service of a petition may be direct or by advertisement in a newspaper with national circulation.

sections.swTitle: Tume Huru ya Uchaguzi na Mipaka

That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Delimitation of electoral units
sections.simplified: (1) There must be two hundred and ninety constituencies for the purposes of the election of the members of the National Assembly provided for in Article 97(1)(a). (2) The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission must review the names and boundaries of constituencies at intervals of not less than eight years, and not more than twelve years, but any review must be completed at least twelve months before a general election of members of Parliament. (3) The Commission must review the number, names and boundaries of wards periodically.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu delimitation of electoral units. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) There shall be two hundred and ninety constituencies for the purposes of the election of the members of the National Assembly provided for in Article 97(1)(a).
(2) The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall review the names and boundaries of constituencies at intervals of not less than eight years, and not more than twelve years, but any review shall be completed at least twelve months before a general election of members of Parliament.
(3) The Commission shall review the number, names and boundaries of wards periodically.
(4) If a general election is to be held within twelve months after the completion of a review by the Commission, the new boundaries shall not take effect for purposes of that election.
(5) The boundaries of each constituency shall be such that the number of inhabitants in the constituency is, as nearly as possible, equal to the population quota, but the number of inhabitants of a constituency may be greater or lesser than the population quota in the manner specified in clause (6) to take account of— (a) geographical features and urban centres;
(b) community of interest, historical, economic and cultural ties; and
(c) means of communication.

(6) The number of inhabitants of a constituency or ward may be greater or lesser than the population quota by a margin of not more than— (a) forty per cent for cities and sparsely populated areas; and
(b) thirty per cent for the other areas.

(7) In reviewing constituency and ward boundaries the Commission shall— (a) consult all interested parties; and
(b) progressively work towards ensuring that the number of inhabitants in each constituency and ward is, as nearly as possible, equal to the population quota.

(8) If necessary, the Commission shall alter the names and boundaries of constituencies, and the number, names and boundaries of wards.
(9) Subject to clauses (1), (2), (3) and (4), the names and details of the boundaries of constituencies and wards determined by the Commission shall be published in the Gazette, and shall come into effect on the dissolution of Parliament first following their publication.
(10) A person may apply to the High Court for review of a decision of the Commission made under this Article.
(11) An application for the review of a decision made under this Article shall be filed within thirty days of the publication of the decision in the Gazette and shall be heard and determined within three months of the date on which it is filed.
(12) For the purposes of this Article, "population quota" means the number obtained by dividing the number of inhabitants of Kenya by the number of constituencies or wards, as applicable, into which Kenya is divided under this Article.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) There must be two hundred and ninety constituencies for the purposes of the election of the members of the National Assembly provided for in Article 97(1)(a). That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Delimitation of electoral units, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission must review the names and boundaries of constituencies at intervals of not less than eight years, and not more than twelve years, but any review must be completed at least twelve months before a general election of members of Parliament. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Delimitation of electoral units, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu delimitation of electoral units.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu delimitation of electoral units.

sections.title: Allocation of party list seats
sections.simplified: (1) Elections for the seats in Parliament provided for under Articles 97(1)(c) and 98(1)(b), (c) and (d), and for the members of county assemblies under 177(1)(b) and (c), must be on the basis of proportional representation by use of party lists. (2) The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission must be responsible for the conduct and supervision of elections for seats provided for under clause (1) and must ensure that— (a) each political party participating in a general election nominates and submits a list of all the persons who would stand elected if the party were to be entitled to all the seats provided for under clause (1), within the time prescribed by national legislation; (b) except in the case of the seats provided for under Article 98(1)(b), each party list comprises the appropriate number of qualified candidates and alternates between male and female candidates in the priority in which they are listed; and (c) except in the case of county assembly seats, each party list reflects the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya. (3) The seats referred to in clause (1) must be allocated to political parties in proportion to the total number of seats won by candidates of the political party at the general election.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu allocation of party list seats. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:

(2) The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall be responsible for the conduct and supervision of elections for seats provided for under clause (1) and shall ensure that— (a) each political party participating in a general election nominates and submits a list of all the persons who would stand elected if the party were to be entitled to all the seats provided for under clause (1), within the time prescribed by national legislation;
(b) except in the case of the seats provided for under Article 98(1)(b), each party list comprises the appropriate number of qualified candidates and alternates between male and female candidates in the priority in which they are listed; and
(c) except in the case of county assembly seats, each party list reflects the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya.

(3) The seats referred to in clause (1) shall be allocated to political parties in proportion to the total number of seats won by candidates of the political party at the general election.

sections.title: Basic requirements for political parties
sections.simplified: (1) Every political party must— (a) have a national character as prescribed by an Act of Parliament; (b) have a democratically elected governing body; (c) promote and uphold national unity; (d) abide by the democratic principles of good governance, promote and practise democracy through regular, fair and free elections within the party; (e) respect the right of all persons to participate in the political process, including minorities and marginalised groups; (f) respect and promote human rights and fundamental freedoms, and gender equality and equity; (g) promote the objects and principles of this Constitution and the rule of law; and (h) subscribe to and observe the code of conduct for political parties. (2) A political party must not— (a) be founded on a religious, linguistic, racial, ethnic, gender or regional basis or seek to engage in advocacy of hatred on any such basis; (b) engage in or encourage violence by, or intimidation of, its members, supporters, opponents or any other person; (c) establish or maintain a paramilitary force, militia or similar organisation; (d) engage in bribery or other forms of corruption; or (e) except as is provided under this Chapter or by an Act of Parliament, accept or use public resources to promote its interests or its candidates in elections.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu basic requirements for political parties. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Every political party must— (a) have a national character as prescribed by an Act of Parliament; (b) have a democratically elected governing body; (c) promote and uphold national unity; (d) abide by the democratic principles of good governance, promote and practise democracy through regular, fair and free elections within the party; (e) respect the right of all persons to participate in the political process, including minorities and marginalised groups; (f) respect and promote human rights and fundamental freedoms, and gender equality and equity; (g) promote the objects and principles of this Constitution and the rule of law; and (h) subscribe to and observe the code of conduct for political parties. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Basic requirements for political parties, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) A political party must not— (a) be founded on a religious, linguistic, racial, ethnic, gender or regional basis or seek to engage in advocacy of hatred on any such basis; (b) engage in or encourage violence by, or intimidation of, its members, supporters, opponents or any other person; (c) establish or maintain a paramilitary force, militia or similar organisation; (d) engage in bribery or other forms of corruption; or (e) except as is provided under this Chapter or by an Act of Parliament, accept or use public resources to promote its interests or its candidates in elections. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Basic requirements for political parties, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu basic requirements for political parties.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu basic requirements for political parties.

sections.title: Legislation on political parties
sections.simplified: Parliament must enact legislation to provide for— (a) the reasonable and equitable allocation of airtime, by State-owned and other specified categories of broadcasting media, to political parties either generally or during election campaigns; (b) the regulation of freedom to broadcast in order to ensure fair election campaigning; (c) the regulation of political parties; (d) the roles and functions of political parties; (e) the registration and supervision of political parties; (f) the establishment and management of a political parties fund; (g) the accounts and audit of political parties; (h) restrictions on the use of public resources to promote the interests of political parties; and (i) any other matters necessary for the management of political parties. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Legislation on political parties, that is how it should be handled.
