sections.title: Establishment of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) There is established a Parliament of Kenya, which must consist of the National Assembly and the Senate. (2) The National Assembly and the Senate must perform your respective functions in accordance with this Constitution.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu establishment of Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) There is established a Parliament of Kenya, which shall consist of the National Assembly and the Senate.
(2) The National Assembly and the Senate shall perform their respective functions in accordance with this Constitution.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) There is established a Parliament of Kenya, which must consist of the National Assembly and the Senate. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Establishment of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The National Assembly and the Senate must perform your respective functions in accordance with this Constitution. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Establishment of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Role of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament makes laws.
sections.swSimplified: Bunge hutunga sheria.
sections.originalText:
(1) The legislative authority of the Republic is derived from the people and, at the national level, is vested in and exercised by Parliament.
(2) Parliament manifests the diversity of the nation, represents the will of the people, and exercises their sovereignty.
(3) Parliament may consider and pass amendments to this Constitution, and alter county boundaries as provided for in this Constitution.
(4) Parliament shall protect this Constitution and promote the democratic governance of the Republic.
(5) No person or body, other than Parliament, has the power to make provision having the force of law in Kenya except under authority conferred by this Constitution or by legislation.
(6) An Act of Parliament, or legislation of a county, that confers on any State organ, State officer or person the authority to make provision having the force of law in Kenya, as contemplated in clause (5), shall expressly specify the purpose and objectives for which that authority is conferred, the limits of the authority, the nature and scope of the law that may be made, and the principles and standards applicable to the law made under the authority.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The legislative authority of the Republic is derived from the people and, at the national level, is vested in and exercised by Parliament. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Role of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament manifests the diversity of the nation, represents the will of the people, and exercises your sovereignty. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Role of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu role of Bunge.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu role of Bunge.

sections.title: Role of the National Assembly
sections.simplified: Represents people in constituencies and oversees national revenue.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kazi ya Bunge la Taifa. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) The National Assembly represents the people of the constituencies and special interests in the National Assembly.
(2) The National Assembly deliberates on and resolves issues of concern to the people.
(3) The National Assembly enacts legislation in accordance with Part 4 of this Chapter.
(4) The National Assembly— (a) determines the allocation of national revenue between the levels of government, as provided in Part 4 of Chapter Twelve;
(b) appropriates funds for expenditure by the national government and other national State organs; and
(c) exercises oversight over national revenue and its expenditure.

(5) The National Assembly— (a) reviews the conduct in office of the President, the Deputy President and other State officers and initiates the process of removing them from office; and
(b) exercises oversight of State organs.

(6) The National Assembly approves declarations of war and extensions of states of emergency.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The National Assembly represents the people of the constituencies and special interests in the National Assembly. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Role of the National Assembly, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The National Assembly deliberates on and resolves issues of concern to the people. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Role of the National Assembly, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kazi ya Bunge la Taifa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kazi ya Bunge la Taifa.

sections.title: Role of the Senate
sections.simplified: Represents the counties and protects their interests.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kazi ya Seneti. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) The Senate represents the counties, and serves to protect the interests of the counties and their governments.
(2) The Senate participates in the law-making function of Parliament by considering, debating and approving Bills concerning counties, as provided in Articles 109 to 113.
(3) The Senate determines the allocation of national revenue among counties, as provided in Article 217, and exercises oversight over national revenue allocated to the county governments.
(4) The Senate participates in the oversight of State officers by considering and determining any resolution to remove the President or Deputy President from office in accordance with Article 145.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The Senate represents the counties, and serves to protect the interests of the counties and your governments. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Role of the Senate, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The Senate participates in the law-making function of Parliament by considering, debating and approving Bills concerning counties, as provided in Articles 109 to 113. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Role of the Senate, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu kazi ya Seneti.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu kazi ya Seneti.

sections.title: Membership of the National Assembly
sections.simplified: (1) The National Assembly consists of— (a) two hundred and ninety members, each elected by the registered voters of single member constituencies; (b) forty-seven women, each elected by the registered voters of the counties, each county constituting a single member constituency; (c) twelve members nominated by parliamentary political parties according to your proportion of members of the National Assembly in accordance with Article 90, to represent special interests including the youth, persons with disabilities and workers; and (d) the Speaker, who is an ex officio member. (2) Nothing in this Article must be construed as excluding You from contesting an election under clause (1)(a).
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uanachama wa Bunge la Taifa. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The National Assembly consists of— (a) two hundred and ninety members, each elected by the registered voters of single member constituencies; (b) forty-seven women, each elected by the registered voters of the counties, each county constituting a single member constituency; (c) twelve members nominated by parliamentary political parties according to your proportion of members of the National Assembly in accordance with Article 90, to represent special interests including the youth, persons with disabilities and workers; and (d) the Speaker, who is an ex officio member. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Membership of the National Assembly, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Nothing in this Article must be construed as excluding You from contesting an election under clause (1)(a). That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Membership of the National Assembly, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu uanachama wa Bunge la Taifa.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uanachama wa Bunge la Taifa.

sections.title: Membership of the Senate
sections.simplified: (1) The Senate consists of— (a) forty-seven members each elected by the registered voters of the counties, each county constituting a single member constituency; (b) sixteen women members who must be nominated by political parties according to your proportion of members of the Senate elected under paragraph (a) in accordance with Article 90; (c) two members, being one man and one woman, representing the youth; (d) two members, being one man and one woman, representing persons with disabilities; and (e) the Speaker, who must be an ex officio member. (2) The members referred to in paragraph (1)(c) and (d) must be elected in accordance with Article 90. (3) Nothing in this Article must be construed as excluding You from contesting an election under clause (1)(a).
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu uanachama wa Seneti. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Membership of the Senate, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The members referred to in paragraph (1)(c) and (d) must be elected in accordance with Article 90. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Membership of the Senate, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu uanachama wa Seneti.

sections.title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as member of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) Unless disqualified under clause (2), You is eligible for election as a member of Parliament if the person— (a) is registered as a voter; (b) satisfies any educational, moral and ethical requirements prescribed by this Constitution or by an Act of Parliament; and (c) is nominated by a political party, or is an independent candidate who is supported— (i) in the case of election to the National Assembly, by at least one thousand registered voters in the constituency; or (ii) in the case of election to the Senate, by at least two thousand registered voters in the county. (2) You is disqualified from being elected a member of Parliament if the person— (a) is a State officer or other public officer, other than a member of Parliament; (b) has, at any time within the five years immediately preceding the date of election, held office as a member of the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission; (c) has not been a citizen of Kenya for at least the ten years immediately preceding the date of election; (d) is a member of a county assembly; (e) is of unsound mind; (f) is an undischarged bankrupt; (g) is subject to a sentence of imprisonment of at least six months, as at the date of registration as a candidate, or at the date of election; or (h) is found, in accordance with any law, to have misused or abused a State office or public office or in any way to have contravened Chapter Six. (3) You is not disqualified under clause (2) unless all possibility of appeal or review of the relevant sentence or decision has been exhausted.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Qualifications and disqualifications for election as member of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Promotion of representation of marginalised groups
sections.swTitle: Promotion of representation of marginalised groups
sections.simplified: Parliament must enact legislation to promote the representation in Parliament of— (a) women; (b) persons with disabilities; (c) youth; (d) ethnic and other minorities; and (e) marginalised communities. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Promotion of representation of marginalised groups, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: Promotion of representation of marginalised groups. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Promotion of representation of marginalised groups, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Election of members of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) A general election of members of Parliament must be held on the second Tuesday in August in every fifth year. (2) Whenever a vacancy occurs in the office of a member of the National Assembly under Article 97(1)(c), or of the Senate under Article 98(1) (b), (c) or (d), the respective Speaker must, within twenty-one days of the occurrence of the vacancy, give notice in writing of the vacancy to— (a) the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission; and (b) the political party on whose party list the member was elected or nominated. (3) A vacancy referred to in clause (2) must, subject to clause (5), be filled in the manner prescribed by an Act of Parliament within twenty-one days of the notification by the respective Speaker.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu election of members of Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A general election of members of Parliament shall be held on the second Tuesday in August in every fifth year.
(2) Whenever a vacancy occurs in the office of a member of the National Assembly under Article 97(1)(c), or of the Senate under Article 98(1) (b), (c) or (d), the respective Speaker shall, within twenty-one days of the occurrence of the vacancy, give notice in writing of the vacancy to— (a) the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission; and
(b) the political party on whose party list the member was elected or nominated.

(3) A vacancy referred to in clause (2) shall, subject to clause (5), be filled in the manner prescribed by an Act of Parliament within twenty-one days of the notification by the respective Speaker.
(4) Whenever a vacancy occurs in the office of a member of the National Assembly elected under Article 97(1)(a) or (b), or of the Senate elected under Article 98(1)(a)— (a) the respective Speaker shall, within twenty-one days after the occurrence of the vacancy, give notice in writing of the vacancy to the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission; and
(b) a by-election shall be held within ninety days of the occurrence of the vacancy, subject to clause (5).

(5) A vacancy referred to in clause (4) shall not be filled within the three months immediately before a general election.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Election of members of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Election of members of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Term of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) The term of each House of Parliament expires on the date of the next general election. (2) When Kenya is at war, Parliament can, by resolution supported in each House by at least two-thirds of all the members of the House, from time to time extend the term of Parliament by not more than six months at a time. (3) The term of Parliament must not be extended under clause (2) for a total of more than twelve months.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu term of Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:

(3) The term of Parliament shall not be extended under clause (2) for a total of more than twelve months.

sections.title: Vacation of office of member of Parliament
sections.swTitle: Vacation of ofisi of member of Bunge
sections.simplified: (1) The office of a member of Parliament becomes vacant— (a) if the member dies; (b) if, during any session of Parliament, the member is absent from eight sittings of the relevant House without permission, in writing, from the Speaker, and is unable to offer a satisfactory explanation for the absence to the relevant committee; (c) if the member is otherwise removed from office under this Constitution or legislation enacted under Article 80; (d) if the member resigns from Parliament in writing to the Speaker; (e) if, having been elected to Parliament— (i) as a member of a political party, the member resigns from that party or is deemed to have resigned from the party as determined in accordance with the legislation contemplated in clause (2); or (ii) as an independent candidate, the member joins a political party; (f) at the end of the term of the relevant House; or (g) if the member becomes disqualified for election to Parliament under Article 99(2)(d) to (h). (2) Parliament must enact legislation providing for the circumstances under which a member of a political party must be deemed, for the purposes of clause (1)(e), to have resigned from the party.

sections.title: Right of recall
sections.simplified: (1) The electorate under Articles 97 and 98 have the right to recall the member of Parliament representing your constituency before the end of the term of the relevant House of Parliament. (2) Parliament must enact legislation to provide for the grounds on which a member can be recalled and the procedure to be followed.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:

(2) Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for the grounds on which a member may be recalled and the procedure to be followed.

sections.title: Determination of questions of membership
sections.simplified: (1) The High Court must hear and determine any question whether— (a) You has been validly elected as a member of Parliament; or (b) the seat of a member has become vacant. (2) A question under clause (1) must be heard and determined within six months of the date of lodging the petition. (3) Parliament must enact legislation to give full effect to this Article.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu determination of questions of membership. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

(b) the seat of a member has become vacant.

(2) A question under clause (1) shall be heard and determined within six months of the date of lodging the petition.
(3) Parliament shall enact legislation to give full effect to this Article.

sections.title: Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) There must be— (a) a Speaker for each House of Parliament, who must be elected by that House in accordance with the Standing Orders, from among persons who are qualified to be elected as members of Parliament but are not such members; and (b) a Deputy Speaker for each House of Parliament, who must be elected by that House in accordance with the Standing Orders, from among the members of that House. (2) The office of Speaker or Deputy Speaker must become vacant— (a) when a new House of Parliament first meets after an election; (b) if the office holder, as a member of the relevant House, vacates office under Article 103; (c) if the relevant House so resolves by resolution supported by the votes of at least two-thirds of its members; or (d) if the office holder resigns from office in a letter addressed to the relevant House.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The office of Speaker or Deputy Speaker must become vacant— (a) when a new House of Parliament first meets after an election; (b) if the office holder, as a member of the relevant House, vacates office under Article 103; (c) if the relevant House so resolves by resolution supported by the votes of at least two-thirds of its members; or (d) if the office holder resigns from office in a letter addressed to the relevant House. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Presiding in Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) At any sitting of a House of Parliament— (a) the Speaker presides; (b) in the absence of the Speaker, the Deputy Speaker presides; and (c) in the absence of the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker, another member of the House elected by the House presides. (2) At a joint sitting of the Houses of Parliament, the Speaker of the National Assembly must preside, assisted by the Speaker of the Senate.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu presiding in Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:

(b) in the absence of the Speaker, the Deputy Speaker presides; and
(c) in the absence of the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker, another member of the House elected by the House presides.

(2) At a joint sitting of the Houses of Parliament, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall preside, assisted by the Speaker of the Senate.

sections.title: Party leaders
sections.simplified: (1) There must be a leader of the majority party and a leader of the minority party. (2) The leader of the majority party must be the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the largest party or coalition of parties. (3) The leader of the minority party must be the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the second largest party or coalition of parties.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu party viongozi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) There shall be a leader of the majority party and a leader of the minority party.
(2) The leader of the majority party shall be the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the largest party or coalition of parties.
(3) The leader of the minority party shall be the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the second largest party or coalition of parties.
(4) The following order of precedence shall be observed in the National Assembly— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly;
(b) the leader of the majority party; and
(c) the leader of the minority party.

sections.title: Exercise of legislative powers
sections.simplified: (1) Parliament must exercise its legislative power through Bills passed by Parliament and assented to by the President. (2) Any Bill can originate in the National Assembly. (3) A Bill not concerning county government is considered only in the National Assembly, and passed in accordance with Article 122 and the Standing Orders of the Assembly.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu exercise of legislative mamlakas. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Parliament shall exercise its legislative power through Bills passed by Parliament and assented to by the President.
(2) Any Bill may originate in the National Assembly.
(3) A Bill not concerning county government is considered only in the National Assembly, and passed in accordance with Article 122 and the Standing Orders of the Assembly.
(4) A Bill concerning county government may originate in the National Assembly or the Senate, and is passed in accordance with Articles 110 to 113, Articles 122 and 123 and the Standing Orders of the Houses.
(5) A Bill may be introduced by any member or committee of the relevant House of Parliament, but a money Bill may be introduced only in the National Assembly in accordance with Article 114.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Parliament must exercise its legislative power through Bills passed by Parliament and assented to by the President. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Exercise of legislative powers, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Any Bill can originate in the National Assembly. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Exercise of legislative powers, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu exercise of legislative mamlakas.

sections.title: Bills concerning county government
sections.simplified: (1) In this Constitution, "a Bill concerning county government" means— (a) a Bill containing provisions affecting the functions and powers of the county governments set out in the Fourth Schedule; (b) a Bill relating to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; and (c) a Bill referred to in Chapter Twelve affecting the finances of county governments. (2) A Bill concerning county governments is— (a) a special Bill, which must be considered under Article 111, if it— (i) relates to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; or (ii) is the annual County Allocation of Revenue Bill referred to in Article 218; or (b) an ordinary Bill, which must be considered under Article 112, in any other case. (3) Before either House considers a Bill, the Speakers of the National Assembly and Senate must jointly resolve any question as to whether it is a Bill concerning counties and, if it is, whether it is a special or an ordinary Bill.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu bills concerning serikali ya kaunti. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) In this Constitution, "a Bill concerning county government" means— (a) a Bill containing provisions affecting the functions and powers of the county governments set out in the Fourth Schedule;
(b) a Bill relating to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; and
(c) a Bill referred to in Chapter Twelve affecting the finances of county governments.

(2) A Bill concerning county governments is— (a) a special Bill, which shall be considered under Article 111, if it— (i) relates to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; or
(ii) is the annual County Allocation of Revenue Bill referred to in Article 218; or

(b) an ordinary Bill, which shall be considered under Article 112, in any other case.

(3) Before either House considers a Bill, the Speakers of the National Assembly and Senate shall jointly resolve any question as to whether it is a Bill concerning counties and, if it is, whether it is a special or an ordinary Bill.
(4) When any Bill concerning county government has been passed by one House of Parliament, the Speaker of that House shall refer it to the Speaker of the other House.
(5) If both Houses pass the Bill in the same form, the Speaker of the House in which the Bill originated shall, within seven days, refer the Bill to the President for assent.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) In this Constitution, "a Bill concerning county government" means— (a) a Bill containing provisions affecting the functions and powers of the county governments set out in the Fourth Schedule; (b) a Bill relating to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; and (c) a Bill referred to in Chapter Twelve affecting the finances of county governments. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Bills concerning county government, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) A Bill concerning county governments is— (a) a special Bill, which must be considered under Article 111, if it— (i) relates to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; or (ii) is the annual County Allocation of Revenue Bill referred to in Article 218; or (b) an ordinary Bill, which must be considered under Article 112, in any other case. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Bills concerning county government, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu bills concerning serikali ya kaunti.

sections.title: Special Bills concerning county governments
sections.swTitle: Special Bills concerning serikali za kaunti
sections.simplified: (1) A special Bill concerning a county government must proceed in the same manner as an ordinary Bill concerning county government, subject to clauses (2) and (3). (2) The National Assembly can amend or veto a special Bill that has been passed by the Senate only by a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of the members of the Assembly. (3) If a resolution in the National Assembly to amend or veto a special Bill fails to pass, the Speaker of the Assembly must, within seven days, refer the Bill, in the form adopted by the Senate, to the President for assent.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu special Bills concerning serikali za kaunti. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A special Bill concerning a county government shall proceed in the same manner as an ordinary Bill concerning county government, subject to clauses (2) and (3).
(2) The National Assembly may amend or veto a special Bill that has been passed by the Senate only by a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of the members of the Assembly.
(3) If a resolution in the National Assembly to amend or veto a special Bill fails to pass, the Speaker of the Assembly shall, within seven days, refer the Bill, in the form adopted by the Senate, to the President for assent.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) A special Bill concerning a county government must proceed in the same manner as an ordinary Bill concerning county government, subject to clauses (2) and (3). That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Special Bills concerning county governments, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The National Assembly can amend or veto a special Bill that has been passed by the Senate only by a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of the members of the Assembly. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Special Bills concerning county governments, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu special Bills concerning serikali za kaunti.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu special Bills concerning serikali za kaunti.

sections.title: Ordinary Bills concerning county governments
sections.swTitle: Ordinary Bills concerning serikali za kaunti
sections.simplified: (1) If one House passes an ordinary Bill concerning counties, and the second House— (a) rejects the Bill, it must be referred to a mediation committee appointed under Article 113; or (b) passes the Bill in an amended form, it must be referred back to the originating House for reconsideration. (2) If, after the originating House has reconsidered a Bill referred back to it under clause (1)(b), that House— (a) passes the Bill as amended, the Speaker of that House must refer the Bill to the President within seven days for assent; or (b) rejects the Bill as amended, the Bill must be referred to a mediation committee under Article 113.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ordinary Bills concerning serikali za kaunti. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:

(2) If, after the originating House has reconsidered a Bill referred back to it under clause (1)(b), that House— (a) passes the Bill as amended, the Speaker of that House shall refer the Bill to the President within seven days for assent; or
(b) rejects the Bill as amended, the Bill shall be referred to a mediation committee under Article 113.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Ordinary Bills concerning county governments, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Mediation committees
sections.simplified: (1) If a Bill is referred to a mediation committee under Article 112, the Speakers of both Houses must appoint a mediation committee consisting of equal numbers of members of each House to attempt to develop a version of the Bill that both Houses will pass. (2) If the mediation committee agrees on a version of the Bill, each House must vote to approve or reject that version of the Bill. (3) If both Houses approve the version of the Bill proposed by the mediation committee, the Speaker of the National Assembly must refer the Bill to the President within seven days for assent.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu mediation committees. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) If a Bill is referred to a mediation committee under Article 112, the Speakers of both Houses shall appoint a mediation committee consisting of equal numbers of members of each House to attempt to develop a version of the Bill that both Houses will pass.
(2) If the mediation committee agrees on a version of the Bill, each House shall vote to approve or reject that version of the Bill.
(3) If both Houses approve the version of the Bill proposed by the mediation committee, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall refer the Bill to the President within seven days for assent.
(4) If the mediation committee fails to agree on a version of the Bill within thirty days, or if a version proposed by the committee is rejected by either House, the Bill is defeated.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) If a Bill is referred to a mediation committee under Article 112, the Speakers of both Houses must appoint a mediation committee consisting of equal numbers of members of each House to attempt to develop a version of the Bill that both Houses will pass. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Mediation committees, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) If the mediation committee agrees on a version of the Bill, each House must vote to approve or reject that version of the Bill. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Mediation committees, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu mediation committees.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu mediation committees.

sections.title: Money Bills
sections.simplified: (1) A money Bill can not deal with any matter other than those listed in the definition of "a money Bill" in clause (3). (2) If, in the opinion of the Speaker of the National Assembly, a motion makes provision for a matter listed in the definition of "a money Bill", the Assembly can proceed only in accordance with the recommendation of the relevant Committee of the Assembly after taking into account the views of the Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance. (3) In this Constitution, "a money Bill" means a Bill, other than a Bill specified in Article 218, that contains provisions dealing with— (a) taxes; (b) the imposition of charges on a public fund or the variation or repeal of any of those charges; (c) the appropriation, receipt, custody, investment or issue of public money; (d) the raising or guaranteeing of any loan or its repayment; or (e) matters incidental to any of those matters.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu money Bills. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) A money Bill can not deal with any matter other than those listed in the definition of "a money Bill" in clause (3). That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Money Bills, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) If, in the opinion of the Speaker of the National Assembly, a motion makes provision for a matter listed in the definition of "a money Bill", the Assembly can proceed only in accordance with the recommendation of the relevant Committee of the Assembly after taking into account the views of the Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Money Bills, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Presidential assent and referral
sections.simplified: (1) Within fourteen days after receipt of a Bill, the President must— (a) assent to the Bill; or (b) refer the Bill back to Parliament for reconsideration by Parliament, noting any reservations that the President has concerning the Bill. (2) If the President refers a Bill back for reconsideration, Parliament can, following the appropriate procedures under this Part— (a) amend the Bill in light of the President’s reservations; or (b) pass the Bill a second time without amendment. (3) If Parliament amends the Bill fully accommodating the President’s reservations, the appropriate Speaker must re-submit it to the President for assent.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Within fourteen days after receipt of a Bill, the President shall— (a) assent to the Bill; or
(b) refer the Bill back to Parliament for reconsideration by Parliament, noting any reservations that the President has concerning the Bill.

(2) If the President refers a Bill back for reconsideration, Parliament may, following the appropriate procedures under this Part— (a) amend the Bill in light of the President’s reservations; or
(b) pass the Bill a second time without amendment.

(3) If Parliament amends the Bill fully accommodating the President’s reservations, the appropriate Speaker shall re-submit it to the President for assent.
(4) Parliament, after considering the President’s reservations, may pass the Bill a second time, without amendment, or with amendments that do not fully accommodate the President’s reservations, by a vote supported— (a) by two-thirds of members of the National Assembly; and
(b) two-thirds of the delegations in the Senate, if it is a Bill that requires the approval of the Senate.

(5) If Parliament has passed a Bill under clause (4)— (a) the appropriate Speaker shall within seven days re-submit it to the President; and
(b) the President shall within seven days assent to the Bill.

(6) If the President does not assent to a Bill or refer it back within the period prescribed in clause (1), or assent to it under clause (5)(b), the Bill shall be taken to have been assented to on the expiry of that period.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Within fourteen days after receipt of a Bill, the President must— (a) assent to the Bill; or (b) refer the Bill back to Parliament for reconsideration by Parliament, noting any reservations that the President has concerning the Bill. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Presidential assent and referral, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) If the President refers a Bill back for reconsideration, Parliament can, following the appropriate procedures under this Part— (a) amend the Bill in light of the President’s reservations; or (b) pass the Bill a second time without amendment. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Presidential assent and referral, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ibara hii.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ibara hii.

sections.title: Coming into force of laws
sections.simplified: (1) A Bill passed by Parliament and assented to by the President must be published in the Gazette as an Act of Parliament within seven days after-assent. (2) Subject to clause (3), an Act of Parliament comes into force on the fourteenth day after its publication in the Gazette, unless the Act stipulates a different date on or time at which it will come into force. (3) An Act of Parliament that confers a direct pecuniary interest on members of Parliament must not come into force until after the next general election of members of Parliament.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu coming into force of sheria. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) A Bill passed by Parliament and assented to by the President shall be published in the Gazette as an Act of Parliament within seven days after-assent.
(2) Subject to clause (3), an Act of Parliament comes into force on the fourteenth day after its publication in the Gazette, unless the Act stipulates a different date on or time at which it will come into force.
(3) An Act of Parliament that confers a direct pecuniary interest on members of Parliament shall not come into force until after the next general election of members of Parliament.
(4) Clause (3) does not apply to an interest that members of Parliament have as members of the public.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Coming into force of laws, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Subject to clause (3), an Act of Parliament comes into force on the fourteenth day after its publication in the Gazette, unless the Act stipulates a different date on or time at which it will come into force. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Coming into force of laws, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Powers, privileges and immunities
sections.simplified: (1) There must be freedom of speech and debate in Parliament. (2) Parliament can, for the purpose of the orderly and effective discharge of the business of Parliament, provide for the powers, privileges and immunities of Parliament, its committees, the leader of the majority party, the leader of the minority party, the chairpersons of committees and members.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Public access and participation
sections.simplified: Public participation.
sections.swSimplified: Public participation.
sections.originalText:
(1) Parliament shall— (a) conduct its business in an open manner, and its sittings and those of its committees shall be in public; and
(b) facilitate public participation and involvement in the legislative and other business of Parliament and its committees.

(2) Parliament may not exclude the public, or any media, from any sitting unless in exceptional circumstances the relevant Speaker has determined that there are justifiable reasons for the exclusion.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Parliament must— (a) conduct its business in an open manner, and its sittings and those of its committees must be in public; and (b) facilitate public participation and involvement in the legislative and other business of Parliament and its committees. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Public access and participation, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament can not exclude the public, or any media, from any sitting unless in exceptional circumstances the relevant Speaker has determined that there are justifiable reasons for the exclusion. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Public access and participation, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Right to petition Parliament
sections.simplified: Right to petition Parliament to consider any matter within its authority.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Every person has a right to petition Parliament to consider any matter within its authority, including to enact, amend or repeal any legislation.
(2) Parliament shall make provision for the procedure for the exercise of this right.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) You has a right to petition Parliament to consider any matter within its authority, including to enact, amend or repeal any legislation. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Right to petition Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament must make provision for the procedure for the exercise of this right. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Right to petition Parliament, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ibara hii.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu ibara hii.

sections.title: Official languages of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) The official languages of Parliament must be Kiswahili, English and Kenyan Sign language, and the business of Parliament can be conducted in English, Kiswahili and Kenyan Sign language. (2) In case of a conflict between different language versions of an Act of Parliament, the version signed by the President must prevail.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu official languages of Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Quorum
sections.simplified: The quorum of Parliament must be– (a) fifty members, in the case of the National Assembly; or (b) fifteen members, in the case of the Senate. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu quorum. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Quorum, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Voting in Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) Except as otherwise provided in this Constitution, any question proposed for decision in either House of Parliament must be determined by a majority of the members in that House, present and voting. (2) On a question proposed for decision in either House— (a) the Speaker has no vote; and (b) in the case of a tie, the question is lost. (3) A member must not vote on any question in which the member has a pecuniary interest.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu kupiga kura in Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Except as otherwise provided in this Constitution, any question proposed for decision in either House of Parliament shall be determined by a majority of the members in that House, present and voting.
(2) On a question proposed for decision in either House— (a) the Speaker has no vote; and
(b) in the case of a tie, the question is lost.

(3) A member shall not vote on any question in which the member has a pecuniary interest.
(4) In reckoning the number of members of a House of Parliament for any purpose of voting in that House, the Speaker of that House shall not be counted as a member.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Except as otherwise provided in this Constitution, any question proposed for decision in either House of Parliament must be determined by a majority of the members in that House, present and voting. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Voting in Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) On a question proposed for decision in either House— (a) the Speaker has no vote; and (b) in the case of a tie, the question is lost. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Voting in Parliament, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Decisions of Senate
sections.simplified: (1) On election, all the members of the Senate who were registered as voters in a particular county must collectively constitute a single delegation for purposes of clause (4) and the member elected under Article 98(1)(a) must be the head of the delegation. (2) When the Senate is to vote on any matter other than a Bill, the Speaker must rule on whether the matter affects or does not affect counties. (3) When the Senate votes on a matter that does not affect counties, each senator has one vote.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu decisions of Seneti. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) On election, all the members of the Senate who were registered as voters in a particular county shall collectively constitute a single delegation for purposes of clause (4) and the member elected under Article 98(1)(a) shall be the head of the delegation.
(2) When the Senate is to vote on any matter other than a Bill, the Speaker shall rule on whether the matter affects or does not affect counties.
(3) When the Senate votes on a matter that does not affect counties, each senator has one vote.
(4) Except as provided otherwise in this Constitution, in any matter in the Senate affecting counties— (a) each county delegation shall have one vote to be cast on behalf of the county by the head of the county delegation or, in the absence of the head of the delegation, by another member of the delegation designated by the head of the delegation;
(b) the person who votes on behalf of a delegation shall determine whether or not to vote in support of, or against, the matter, after consulting the other members of the delegation; and
(c) the matter is carried only if it is supported by a majority of all the delegations.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) On election, all the members of the Senate who were registered as voters in a particular county must collectively constitute a single delegation for purposes of clause (4) and the member elected under Article 98(1)(a) must be the head of the delegation. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Decisions of Senate, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) When the Senate is to vote on any matter other than a Bill, the Speaker must rule on whether the matter affects or does not affect counties. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Decisions of Senate, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Committees and Standing Orders
sections.simplified: (1) Each House of Parliament can establish committees, and must make Standing Orders for the orderly conduct of its proceedings, including the proceedings of its committees. (2) Parliament can establish joint committees consisting of members of both Houses and can jointly regulate the procedure of those committees. (3) The proceedings of either House are not invalid just because of— (a) a vacancy in its membership; or (b) the presence or participation of You not entitled to be present at, or to participate in, the proceedings of the House.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Each House of Parliament may establish committees, and shall make Standing Orders for the orderly conduct of its proceedings, including the proceedings of its committees.
(2) Parliament may establish joint committees consisting of members of both Houses and may jointly regulate the procedure of those committees.
(3) The proceedings of either House are not invalid just because of— (a) a vacancy in its membership; or
(b) the presence or participation of any person not entitled to be present at, or to participate in, the proceedings of the House.

(4) When a House of Parliament considers any appointment for which its approval is required under this Constitution or an Act of Parliament— (a) the appointment shall be considered by a committee of the relevant House;
(b) the committee’s recommendation shall be tabled in the House for approval; and
(c) the proceedings of the committee and the House shall be in public.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Each House of Parliament can establish committees, and must make Standing Orders for the orderly conduct of its proceedings, including the proceedings of its committees. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Committees and Standing Orders, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Parliament can establish joint committees consisting of members of both Houses and can jointly regulate the procedure of those committees. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Committees and Standing Orders, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu ibara hii.

sections.title: Power to call for evidence
sections.simplified: (1) Either House of Parliament, and any of its committees, has power to summon You to appear before it for the purpose of giving evidence or providing information. (2) For the purposes of clause (1), a House of Parliament and any of its committees has the same powers as the High Court— (a) to enforce the attendance of witnesses and examine them on oath, affirmation or otherwise; (b) to compel the production of documents; and (c) to issue a commission or request to examine witnesses abroad.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu power to call for evidence. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Either House of Parliament, and any of its committees, has power to summon any person to appear before it for the purpose of giving evidence or providing information.
(2) For the purposes of clause (1), a House of Parliament and any of its committees has the same powers as the High Court— (a) to enforce the attendance of witnesses and examine them on oath, affirmation or otherwise;
(b) to compel the production of documents; and
(c) to issue a commission or request to examine witnesses abroad.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Either House of Parliament, and any of its committees, has power to summon You to appear before it for the purpose of giving evidence or providing information. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Power to call for evidence, that is how it should be handled.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Power to call for evidence, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Location of sittings of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) A sitting of either House can be held at any place within Kenya and can commence at any time that the House appoints. (2) Whenever a new House is elected, the President, by notice in the Gazette, must appoint the place and date for the first sitting of the new House, which must be not more than thirty days after the election.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu location of sittings of Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Parliamentary Service Commission
sections.simplified: (1) There is established the Parliamentary Service Commission. (2) The Commission consists of— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly, as chairperson; (b) a vice-chairperson elected by the Commission from the members appointed under paragraph (c); (c) seven members appointed by Parliament from among its members of whom— (i) four must be nominated equally from both Houses by the party or coalition of parties forming the national government, of whom at least two must be women; and (ii) three must be nominated by the parties not forming the national government, at least one of whom must be nominated from each House and at least one of whom must be a woman; and (d) one man and one woman appointed by Parliament from among persons who are experienced in public affairs, but are not members of Parliament. (3) The Clerk of the Senate must be the Secretary to the Commission.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu tume ya Huduma za Bunge. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) There is established the Parliamentary Service Commission.
(2) The Commission consists of— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly, as chairperson;
(b) a vice-chairperson elected by the Commission from the members appointed under paragraph (c);
(c) seven members appointed by Parliament from among its members of whom— (i) four shall be nominated equally from both Houses by the party or coalition of parties forming the national government, of whom at least two shall be women; and
(ii) three shall be nominated by the parties not forming the national government, at least one of whom shall be nominated from each House and at least one of whom shall be a woman; and

(d) one man and one woman appointed by Parliament from among persons who are experienced in public affairs, but are not members of Parliament.

(3) The Clerk of the Senate shall be the Secretary to the Commission.
(4) A member of the Commission shall vacate office— (a) if the person is a member of Parliament— (i) at the end of the term of the House of which the person is a member; or
(ii) if the person ceases to be a member of Parliament; or

(b) if the person is an appointed member, on revocation of the person’s appointment by Parliament.

(5) Despite clause (4), when the term of a House of Parliament ends, a member of the Commission appointed under clause (2)(c) shall continue in office until a new member has been appointed in the member’s place by the next House.
(6) The Commission is responsible for— (a) providing services and facilities to ensure the efficient and effective functioning of Parliament;
(b) constituting offices in the parliamentary service, and appointing and supervising office holders;
(c) preparing annual estimates of expenditure of the parliamentary service and submitting them to the National Assembly for approval, and exercising budgetary control over the service;
(d) undertaking, singly or jointly with other relevant organisations, programmes to promote the ideals of parliamentary democracy; and
(e) performing other functions— (i) necessary for the well-being of the members and staff of Parliament; or
(ii) prescribed by national legislation.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) There is established the Parliamentary Service Commission. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Parliamentary Service Commission, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The Commission consists of— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly, as chairperson; (b) a vice-chairperson elected by the Commission from the members appointed under paragraph (c); (c) seven members appointed by Parliament from among its members of whom— (i) four must be nominated equally from both Houses by the party or coalition of parties forming the national government, of whom at least two must be women; and (ii) three must be nominated by the parties not forming the national government, at least one of whom must be nominated from each House and at least one of whom must be a woman; and (d) one man and one woman appointed by Parliament from among persons who are experienced in public affairs, but are not members of Parliament. That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Parliamentary Service Commission, that is how it should be handled.
swExamples: Ibara hii inaonyesha sheria ya msingi kuhusu tume ya Huduma za Bunge.
Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu tume ya Huduma za Bunge.

sections.title: Clerks and staff of Parliament
sections.simplified: (1) There must be a Clerk for each House of Parliament, appointed by the Parliamentary Service Commission with the approval of the relevant House. (2) The offices of the Clerks and offices of members of the staff of the Clerks must be offices in the Parliamentary Service.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Principles of executive authority
sections.simplified: Executive authority is exercised for the service of the people.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) Executive authority derives from the people of Kenya and shall be exercised in accordance with this Constitution.
(2) Executive authority shall be exercised in a manner compatible with the principle of service to the people of Kenya, and for their well-being and benefit.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) Executive authority derives from the people of Kenya and must be exercised in accordance with this Constitution. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Principles of executive authority, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) Executive authority must be exercised in a manner compatible with the principle of service to the people of Kenya, and for your well-being and benefit. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Principles of executive authority, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: The National Executive
sections.simplified: (1) The national executive of the Republic comprises the President, the Deputy President and the rest of the Cabinet. (2) The composition of the national executive must reflect the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Authority of the President
sections.simplified: The President is the symbol of national unity and Head of State.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The President— (a) is the Head of State and Government; (b) exercises the executive authority of the Republic, with the assistance of the Deputy President and Cabinet Secretaries; (c) is the Commander-in-Chief of the Kenya Defence Forces; (d) is the chairperson of the National Security Council; and (e) is a symbol of national unity. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Authority of the President, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The President must— (a) respect, uphold and safeguard this Constitution; (b) safeguard the sovereignty of the Republic; (c) promote and enhance the unity of the nation; (d) promote respect for the diversity of the people and communities of Kenya; and (e) ensure the protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms and the rule of law. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Authority of the President, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Functions of the President
sections.simplified: (1) The President must— (a) address the opening of each newly elected Parliament; (b) address a special sitting of Parliament once every year and can address Parliament at any other time; and (c) once every year— (i) report, in an address to the nation, on all the measures taken and the progress achieved in the realisation of the national values, referred to in Article 10; (ii) publish in the Gazette the details of the measures and progress under sub-paragraph (i); and (iii) submit a report for debate to the National Assembly on the progress made in fulfilling the international obligations of the Republic. (2) The President must nominate and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appoint, and can dismiss— (a) the Cabinet Secretaries, in accordance with Article 152; (b) the Attorney-General, in accordance with Article 156; (c) the Secretary to the Cabinet in accordance with Article 154; (d) Principal Secretaries in accordance with Article 155; (e) high commissioners, ambassadors and diplomatic and consular representatives; and (f) in accordance with this Constitution, any other State or public officer whom this Constitution requires or empowers the President to appoint or dismiss. (3) The President must— (a) chair Cabinet meetings; (b) direct and co-ordinate the functions of ministries and government departments; and (c) by a decision published in the Gazette, assign responsibility for the implementation and administration of any Act of Parliament to a Cabinet Secretary, to the extent not inconsistent with any Act of Parliament.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Functions of the President, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Power of mercy
sections.simplified: (1) On the petition of You, the President can exercise a power of mercy in accordance with the advice of the Advisory Committee established under clause (2), by— (a) granting a free or conditional pardon to You convicted of an offence; (b) postponing the carrying out of a punishment, either for a specified or indefinite period; (c) substituting a less severe form of punishment; or (d) remitting all or part of a punishment. (2) There must be an Advisory Committee on the Power of Mercy, comprising— (a) the Attorney-General; (b) the Cabinet Secretary responsible for correctional services; and (c) at least five other members as prescribed by an Act of Parliament, none of whom can be a State officer or in public service. (3) Parliament must enact legislation to provide for— (a) the tenure of the members of the Advisory Committee; (b) the procedure of the Advisory Committee; and (c) criteria that must be applied by the Advisory Committee in formulating its advice.

That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Power of mercy, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Exercise of presidential powers during temporary incumbency
sections.swTitle: Exercise of raisial mamlakas during temporary incumbency
sections.simplified: (1) You who holds the office of President or who is authorised in terms of this Constitution to exercise the powers of the President— (a) during the period commencing on the date of the first vote in a presidential election, and ending when the newly elected President assumes office; or (b) while the President is absent or incapacitated, or at other times contemplated in Article 147(3), can not exercise the powers of the President specified in clause (2). (2) The powers referred to in clause (1) are— (a) the nomination or appointment of the judges of the superior courts; (b) the nomination or appointment of any other public officer whom this Constitution or legislation requires the President to appoint; (c) the nomination or appointment or dismissal of Cabinet Secretaries and other State or Public officers; (d) the nomination or appointment or dismissal of a high commissioner, ambassador, or diplomatic or consular representative; (e) the power of mercy; and (f) the authority to confer honours in the name of the people and the Republic.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu exercise of raisial mamlakas during temporary incumbency. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Exercise of presidential powers during temporary incumbency, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) The powers referred to in clause (1) are— (a) the nomination or appointment of the judges of the superior courts; (b) the nomination or appointment of any other public officer whom this Constitution or legislation requires the President to appoint; (c) the nomination or appointment or dismissal of Cabinet Secretaries and other State or Public officers; (d) the nomination or appointment or dismissal of a high commissioner, ambassador, or diplomatic or consular representative; (e) the power of mercy; and (f) the authority to confer honours in the name of the people and the Republic. sections.title: Decisions of the President
sections.simplified: A decision of the President in the performance of any function of the President under this Constitution must be in writing and must bear the seal and signature of the President. It also guides what officials must or must not do for you.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

sections.title: Election of the President
sections.simplified: (1) The President must be elected by registered voters in a national election conducted in accordance with this Constitution and any Act of Parliament regulating presidential elections. (2) An election of the President must be held— (a) on the same day as a general election of Members of Parliament, being the second Tuesday in August, in every fifth year; or (b) in the circumstances contemplated in Article 146.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:

(2) An election of the President shall be held— (a) on the same day as a general election of Members of Parliament, being the second Tuesday in August, in every fifth year; or
(b) in the circumstances contemplated in Article 146.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) The President must be elected by registered voters in a national election conducted in accordance with this Constitution and any Act of Parliament regulating presidential elections. sections.title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as President
sections.simplified: (1) You qualifies for nomination as a presidential candidate if the person— (a) is a citizen by birth; (b) is qualified to stand for election as a member of Parliament; (c) is nominated by a political party, or is an independent candidate; and (d) is nominated by not fewer than two thousand voters from each of a majority of the counties. (2) You is not qualified for nomination as a presidential candidate if the person— (a) owes allegiance to a foreign state; or (b) is a public officer, or is acting in any State or other public office. (3) Clause (2)(b) must not apply to— (a) the President; (b) the Deputy President; or (c) a member of Parliament.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu ibara hii. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.

That would break this protection, and they can challenge it. Under this part on Qualifications and disqualifications for election as President, that is how it should be handled.

sections.title: Procedure at presidential election
sections.simplified: (1) If only one candidate for President is nominated, that candidate must be declared elected. (2) If two or more candidates for President are nominated, an election must be held in each constituency. (3) In a presidential election— (a) all persons registered as voters for the purposes of parliamentary elections are entitled to vote; (b) the poll must be taken by secret ballot on the day specified in Article 101(1) at the time, in the places and in the manner prescribed under an Act of Parliament; and (c) after counting the votes in the polling stations, the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission must tally and verify the count and declare the result.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu procedure at raisial uchaguzi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) If only one candidate for President is nominated, that candidate shall be declared elected.
(2) If two or more candidates for President are nominated, an election shall be held in each constituency.
(3) In a presidential election— (a) all persons registered as voters for the purposes of parliamentary elections are entitled to vote;
(b) the poll shall be taken by secret ballot on the day specified in Article 101(1) at the time, in the places and in the manner prescribed under an Act of Parliament; and
(c) after counting the votes in the polling stations, the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall tally and verify the count and declare the result.

(4) A candidate shall be declared elected as President if the candidate receives— (a) more than half of all the votes cast in the election; and
(b) at least twenty-five per cent of the votes cast in each of more than half of the counties.

(5) If no candidate is elected, a fresh election shall be held within thirty days after the previous election and in that fresh election the only candidates shall be— (a) the candidate, or the candidates, who received the greatest number of votes; and
(b) the candidate, or the candidates, who received the second greatest number of votes.

(6) If more than one candidate receives the greatest number of votes, clause (5)(b) shall not apply and the only candidates in the fresh election shall be those contemplated in clause (5)(a).
(7) The candidate who receives the most votes in the fresh election shall be declared elected as President.
(8) A presidential election shall be cancelled and a new election held if— (a) no person has been nominated as a candidate before the expiry of the period set for the delivery of nominations;
(b) a candidate for election as President or Deputy President dies on or before the scheduled election date; or
(c) a candidate who would have been entitled to be declared elected as President, dies before being declared elected as President.

(9) A new presidential election under clause (8) shall be held within sixty days after the date set for the previous presidential election.
(10) Within seven days after the presidential election, the chairperson of the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall— (a) declare the result of the election; and
(b) deliver a written notification of the result to the Chief Justice and the incumbent President.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) If only one candidate for President is nominated, that candidate must be declared elected. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Procedure at presidential election, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) If two or more candidates for President are nominated, an election must be held in each constituency. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Procedure at presidential election, that is how it should be handled.

Mfano wa maisha ya kila siku: sheria hii inatumika unapoona tatizo linalohusu procedure at raisial uchaguzi.

sections.title: Death before assuming office
sections.simplified: (1) If a President-elect dies after being declared elected as President, but before assuming office— (a) the Deputy President-elect must be sworn in as acting President on the date on which the President-elect would otherwise have been sworn-in; and (b) a fresh election to the office of President must be held within sixty days after the death of the President-elect. (2) If the Deputy President-elect dies before assuming office, the office of the Deputy President must be declared vacant on the assumption of office by the person declared elected as the President. (3) If both the persons declared elected as the President and the Deputy President die before assuming office— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly must act as President from the date on which the President-elect would otherwise have been sworn-in; and (b) a fresh presidential election must be conducted within sixty days after the second death.
sections.swSimplified: Ibara hii inaeleza kwa ufupi kuhusu death before assuming ofisi. Soma maandishi rasmi hapa chini kwa maelezo kamili.
sections.originalText:
(1) If a President-elect dies after being declared elected as President, but before assuming office— (a) the Deputy President-elect shall be sworn in as acting President on the date on which the President-elect would otherwise have been sworn-in; and
(b) a fresh election to the office of President shall be held within sixty days after the death of the President-elect.

(2) If the Deputy President-elect dies before assuming office, the office of the Deputy President shall be declared vacant on the assumption of office by the person declared elected as the President.
(3) If both the persons declared elected as the President and the Deputy President die before assuming office— (a) the Speaker of the National Assembly shall act as President from the date on which the President-elect would otherwise have been sworn-in; and
(b) a fresh presidential election shall be conducted within sixty days after the second death.
examples: Kamau is in a real situation: (1) If a President-elect dies after being declared elected as President, but before assuming office— (a) the Deputy President-elect must be sworn in as acting President on the date on which the President-elect would otherwise have been sworn-in; and (b) a fresh election to the office of President must be held within sixty days after the death of the President-elect. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Death before assuming office, that is how it should be handled.

Achieng is in a real situation: (2) If the Deputy President-elect dies before assuming office, the office of the Deputy President must be declared vacant on the assumption of office by the person declared elected as the President. That fits this protection and should happen without being stopped. Under this part on Death before assuming office, that is how it should be handled.
